On the other hand, the VPD distribution of spectroscopic members for Carina II and Reticulum II seems to represent well the overall population as determined by our procedure. In fact, our results match within 1 those in , being ( _ , _ ) = ( 1.81 0.08 , 0.14 0.08 ) for Carina II and ( _ , _ ) = ( 2.34 0.12 , 1.31 0.13 ) for Reticulum II.

sources.summary: The paper presents first detailed abundances for Carina II, showing it is a galaxy with dwarf-galaxy abundance trends, Ba scatter, and declining Mg/Ca from varied supernova enrichment.

quotes.quote: We present the first detailed elemental abundances in the ultra-faint Magellanic satellite galaxies Carina II (Car II) and Carina III (Car III). With high-resolution Magellan/MIKE spectroscopy, we determined abundances of nine stars in Car II including the first abundances of an RR Lyrae star in an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy; and two stars in Car III. The chemical abundances demonstrate that both systems are clearly galaxies and not globular clusters. The stars in these galaxies mostly display abundance trends matching those of other similarly faint dwarf galaxies: enhanced but declining [ /Fe ] ratios, iron-peak elements matching the stellar halo, and unusually low neutron-capture element abundances.

We detect a large Ba scatter in Car II, likely due to inhomogeneous enrichment by low-mass AGB star winds. The most striking abundance trend is for [ Mg/Ca ] in Car II, which decreases from +0.4 to 0.4 and indicates clear variation in the initial progenitor masses of enriching core-collapse supernovae.

sources.summary: The paper models Carina II as an ultra-faint LMC satellite with recorded chemical abundances, stellar mass near 10^4 solar masses, and chemical evolution similar to Milky Way UFDs.

quotes.quote: In this work, we focus on modelling the chemical evolution of two spectroscopically confirmed UFDs -- Carina II (Car II) and Reticulum (Ret II), both satellites of the LMC Bechtol2015, patel2020, Battaglia2022 . Car II - along with Carina III - is generally believed to be very understood from the analysis in Li2018 and Ji2020 , whereas Ret II is chosen from its unique process enrichment early in its formation. We select these two satellite candidates among other UFDs for our analysis as their formation history is well recorded, as well as their chemical abundance measurements. Car II is a satellite of the LMC and was discovered by the Magellanic SatelLites Survey (MagLites) while exploring some of the unknown regions around the Magellanic system Torrealba2018 . Car II is estimated to have a stellar mass M_ 10^ 4 M _ Sales2017 .

Analysis of the elemental abundances within Car II confirmed that this galaxy followed a similar chemical evolution as the MW UFDs, and there is no sign of anti-correlation between light element abundances, confirming that Car II stars had not been part of any disrupted globular cluster Ji2020 .

sources.summary: Carina II is one of the newly found south-pole dwarfs; the paper uses spectroscopy and RR Lyrae information to define 19 bright members for Gaia astrometry.

quotes.quote: The most recently-found dwarfs (Carina II, Carina III, and Hydrus I) were identified in imaging surveys near the south celestial pole that are not currently public, so for those objects I use the coordinates and magnitudes provided in the discovery papers.

In total, there are 22 known member stars and 23 non-members in Ret II at g < 20 . li18 medium-resolution spectroscopy of Carina II (Car II) with Magellan/IMACS, AAT/AAOmega, and VLT/FLAMES and found 18 member stars. Time-series imaging by torrealba18 identified 5 RR Lyrae variables in the field, of which 3 are at the correct distance to be members of Car II. Two of the Car II RR Lyrae were spectroscopically confirmed as members by li18 , and I assume that the third star is a member as well. The 19 known Car II members are all brighter than g=20 , as are a total of 8 confirmed non-members in the field.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Car 2 as a likely former LMC satellite, notes it can remain bound even at low LMC mass, and says another simulation-based technique agrees.

quotes.quote: We study the orbits of ultra-faint dwarf galaxies in the combined presence of the Milky Way and LMC and we find 6 dwarfs which were likely accreted with the LMC (Car 2, Car 3, Hor 1, Hyi 1, Phe 2, Ret 2), in addition to the SMC, representing strong evidence of dwarf galaxy group infall. Interestingly, some of the LMC satellites (Hor 1, Car 2, Car 3, and the SMC) have a non-zero probability of being bound even with the lowest LMC mass considered suggesting that these sit deep in the potential of the LMC. Car 2 requires the lowest LMC mass in order to bind it, consistent with its high probability of being bound even for a low LMC mass (see fig:pbound ). Using a different technique based on a single cosmological simulation, they identified Car 2, Car 3, Hor 1, and Hyi 1 as likely LMC satellites, in agreement with this work. In addition to the SMC, we identify six dwarf galaxies as being highly likely bound to the Cloud previously: Car 2, Car 3, Hor 1, Hyi 1, Phe 2, and Ret 2.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes Carina II without the halo scale-radius prior and notes its low J-Factor relative to distance scaling helped motivate more general scaling relations.

quotes.quote: To quantify the effect of this prior, we re-analyze several galaxies (Bo o tes I, Carina II, Reticulum II, Ursa Major II, Draco, and Leo II) without this prior and expand the r_s prior range ( 3 < _ 10 r_s/ kpc < 1.4 ). While there is no change for the Draco values (the posterior favors larger r_s values) all other galaxies show an increase in the J-Factor and most show a decrease in the D-Factor. This affect is generally larger in the ultra-faint galaxies as the halo properties are more likely to be prior dominated.

One of the recently discovered dSphs, Carina II, contained a significantly lower J-Factor than the distance scaling prediction Li2018ApJ...857..145L and led us to explore more general scaling relations.

sources.summary: The paper selects 18 Carina II members from Li spectroscopy, finds a clear proper-motion clump, and reports agreement with Simon, Kallivayalil, and Massari.

quotes.quote: Carina II We use the catalog of Li_18a which has binary probabilities. The 18 member stars we select form a clear clump in proper motion space supported by stars w/o spectroscopy. Our motion agrees within 1 with Simon_18 , Kallivayalil_18 and Massari_18 .

sources.summary: The paper adds Gaia member stars for Car2 and finds its positions and velocities clearly consistent with probable membership in the infalling LMC group.

quotes.quote: In Fig. fig:selection1 we show the case for Car2 in which we successfully add 14 new members to the spectroscopic sample thereby decreasing the final errors by a factor of 2.

At least 4 dwarfs show clearly consistent positions and velocity measurements compared to our predictions: Hyi1, Car3, Car2 and Hor1 (red labels), presenting a compelling case of probable membership to the LMC group.
Using the resulting 6D velocities, we find that 4 of these galaxies (Hor1, Car2, Car3 and Hyi1) have come in with the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Car2 as an ultrafaint LMC-system member; its orbital consistency and low inner density favor Magellanic association.

quotes.quote: Recent Gaia proper motions confirm new members of the LMC-system in addition to the previously known SMC, including two classical dwarf galaxies ( > 10^5 ; Carina and Fornax) as well as several ultra-faint dwarfs (Car2, Car3, Hor1, and Hyd1).
With the new 6D information, kallivayalil2018MCsats confirmed a likely association for 4 ultrafaints: Car2, Car3, Hor1 and Hyd1.

From that perspective, the likely associations for Hyd1 with = 4.7 km/s and Car2 with = 5.9 km/s, both within 100 pc, or the possibly associated Hyd2 with = 6.2 km/s, seem favored compared to the more dense Car3, Hor1, or Dra2 with 8 km/s at 50 pc data from simon2019UFDs .

sources.summary: Carina II is described as just outside the VPOS area, probably formerly bound to the LMC, and the exception among mostly VPOS-aligned LMC-associated systems.

quotes.quote: We further note that there are systems such as Carina II (and marginally Draco II, Segue 1, but also Leo I and II at greater distances) which, based on their _ VPOS ^ meas , are located just outside the VPOS area (see again Fig. fig:orb_poles ). In particular, Carina II is with high probability a former satellite of the LMC Battaglia2022 , which instead is safely on the VPOS.
The LMC associated systems have orbital poles mostly aligned with the VPOS (except for Carina II, which however is just outside the _ inVPOS area; see Fig. fig:orb_poles ).

gmw_00003381.name: NGC 1380
gmw_00003381.overview: NGC 1380 is an unbarred lenticular galaxy [arXiv:2201.06704] in the Fornax cluster [arXiv:2203.15822] that features a rotating circumnuclear disk [arXiv:2206.09043], a kinematically decoupled core [arXiv:0710.5529], and an optically thick central dust lane [arXiv:0710.5529]. It hosts a central supermassive black hole [arXiv:1812.07582, arXiv:2206.09043] and exhibits faint H-alpha emission indicative of trace star formation [arXiv:1906.08187].

sources.summary: ALMA finds NGC 1380 is CO-bright with a dust-coincident rotating disk, double-horned velocity profile, and a marginal central high-velocity signature.

quotes.quote: The bright CO(2 1) emission seen in NGC 1332 , NGC 1380 , NGC 3258 , NGC 3268 , and NGC 6861 directly coincides with optically thick dust disks as seen in the structure maps of the corresponding optical HST images. Inspecting their image cubes reveals clear rotation about the nuclear continuum sources. To visualize their rotational properties, we integrated the flux densities in each channel over the elliptical regions coinciding with the optical dust disks. These CO velocity profiles (Figure  pvd_velchan ) exhibit nearly symmetric double-horned features characteristic of rotating disks with emission ranging between 300 and 500 km s ^ 1 from v_ sys .

We also constructed a position-velocity diagram (PVD; also shown in Figure  pvd_velchan ) for each galaxy by extracting a slice along the major axis as determined from the average disk position angle (measured in kin ). The extraction widths were set to the projected CLEAN beam FWHM along the disk major axis. A central rise in the PVD envelope to high velocity at small radius, which indicates a massive, compact object at the disk center, is unambiguously observed only in NGC 1332 and NGC 3258 , with a marginal detection in NGC 1380 within the central 0 1 . The PVD for NGC 6861 shows a 2 wide hole in CO emission.

sources.summary: The paper models FCC 167 as a dust-lane early-type galaxy with nested ionized and molecular gas, star formation, AGN-like central ionization, and a ring-like dust/gas geometry lacking small grains.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: FCC 167 ( _ J2000 =03^h36^m27.55^s , _ J2000 =-34 58^ 33.88^ , D=18.8 Mpc) is part of the Fornax Cluster and was observed in the context of the Fornax 3D project (F3D, PI: M.

quotes.quote: We therefore model the various components of such a galaxy (FCC 167). We reconstruct its recent history, and investigate the possible fate of the dust lane. MUSE and ALMA observations reveal a nested ISM structure. An ionised-gas disk pervades the central regions of FCC 167, including those occupied by the main dust lane.

Inward of the dust lane, we also find a disk/ring of cold molecular gas where stars are forming and HII regions contribute to the ionised-gas emission. Further in, the gas ionisation points towards an active galactic nucleus and the fuelling of a central supermassive black hole from its surrounding ionised and molecular reservoir. Observational constraints and radiative transfer models suggest the dust and gas are distributed in a ring-like geometry and the dust mix lacks small grains.

sources.summary: The paper maps FCC 167 stellar populations and IMF in two dimensions, finding metallicity and IMF structures differ and may trace different orbital components.

quotes.quote: We present here a two-dimensional stellar population analysis of the massive lenticular galaxy FCC 167 (NGC 1380) as part of the Fornax3D project. Using a newly developed stellar population fitting scheme, we derive a full two-dimensional IMF map of an early-type galaxy. This two-dimensional analysis allows us go further than a radial analysis, showing how the metallicity changes along a disc-like structure while the IMF follows a distinct, less disky distribution. Thus, our findings indicate that metallicity cannot be the sole driver of the observed radial IMF variations. In addition, a comparison with the orbital decomposition shows suggestive evidence of a coupling between stellar population properties and the internal dynamical structure of FCC 167, where metallicity and IMF maps seem to track the distribution of cold and warm orbits, respectively.

The aim of this work is to take a step further in the observational characterization of the IMF by analyzing its two-dimensional (2D) variation in the massive ( M_B=-20.3 ), fast-rotating ETG FCC 167 (NGC 1380), as part of the Fornax 3D project (F3D). Taking advantage of the unparalleled capabilities of the Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) integral-field spectrograph Bacon10 , we present here a 2D analysis of the stellar population properties of FCC 167, showing for the first time the IMF map of a massive ETG.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 1380 with Fornax3D/MUSE data, finding a lenticular galaxy with a hot inner stellar halo from a massive ancient merger about 10 Gyr ago.

quotes.quote: NGC 1380 (FCC 167) and NGC 1427 (FCC276) are two galaxies in the Fornax cluster observed as part of the FDS Iodice2019a and Fornax3D survey Sarzi2018 . NGC 1380 is a lenticular galaxy located on the north-western side of the cluster at 40 arcmin from the brightest central galaxy (BCG) NGC 1399, and at a distance of 21.2 Mpc from us Blakeslee2009 . NGC 1380 has a total band magnitude of m_r = 9.27 and a half-light radius of R_e = 56.2 = 5.8 kpc.

Through the Fornax3D project, NGC 1380 was observed with three MUSE pointings out to a galactocentric radius of 150 along the major axis and up to 40 along the minor axis (Figure fig:bestfit ). We discovered that the two early-type galaxies NGC 1380 and NGC 1427 in the Fornax cluster both underwent an ancient massive merger, and quantified its stellar mass and merger time. We further found that the last merger of NGC 1380 ended 10 Gyr ago based on the stellar age distribution of its re-grown dynamically cold disk, in a way comparable to how the last massive merger time of the Milky Way could be inferred Belokurov2020 .

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes NGC 1380 MUSE planetary-nebula data, finding a well-defined PNLF distance modulus and a low but non-negligible chance that its brightest PN is a superposition.

quotes.quote: To illustrate the effect of PN superpositions on the analysis of MUSE data, we reanalyzed the data cube of the Fornax lenticular galaxy NGC 1380. The PNLF of this galaxy has been published multiple times -- first from a narrow-band imaging survey by Feldmeier+07 , then from the MUSE observations of Spriggs+20, Spriggs+21 , and most recently, from a reanalysis of the Spriggs+20 data cube using differential emission-line filtering DELF; Roth+21 . While this dataset may not be ideal for determining an absolute distance to the galaxy -- such a measurement requires careful standard star observations and the presence of at least one bright point-source in the field for the photometric aperture correction -- it is more than sufficient for a differential analysis of the data.

From the figure, it is clear that both solutions yield the same most-likely distance modulus, (m-M)_0 30.97 assuming a foreground extinction of E(B-V) = 0.015 ; Schlafly+11 . This is not unexpected, as the shape of the galaxy's PNLF cutoff is extremely well defined by the 50 PNe in the top 1 mag of the luminosity function. But in a conventional analysis, NGC 1380's distance modulus cannot be greater than (m-M)_0 31.1 , as a larger distance would imply an \ 5007 luminosity for the brightest PN that is greater than M^* .

sources.summary: The paper reports that NGC 1380 stellar-rotation position angles agree between methods, uses [O I] to locate its nucleus, and finds an irregular velocity-dispersion structure.

quotes.quote: By comparing the PA values measured with the radial velocity maps and those measured with the tomograms, we noted that both methods match for IC 1459, NGC 1380, NGC 1404 and NGC 4546 (PA _ tom PA _ Vr < 7 ^o ).

In NGC 1380, we used the image of the [ O I ] 6300 line to set the position of the nucleus. We assumed that the nucleus of NGC 1404 is in the photometric centre of the stellar structure (paper I). The positions of the AGN (or the stellar structure) of the galaxies are shown in Table tab_mapa_vel_radial_stellar . The profiles are shown in Fig. perfil_stellar_kin_1 , where the radial velocities are shown with respect to the heliocentric radial velocity at the kinematic centre of the galaxies. figure 1 We proposed in paper I that these seven galaxies have stellar structures in rotation. Both the maps and the profiles of, at least, six objects suggest a rotating structure. Note that there is a well-defined rotation in the 1D profile along the rotating stellar structures, while in the perpendicular direction, the radial velocities are nearly constant near the nucleus. In NGC 1404, the 1D profile along the PA shows a sinusoidal behaviour. By comparing the radial velocity map of this object, shown in Fig. perfil_stellar_kin_2 , with the radial velocity map of 2014MNRAS.441..274S , which has a larger FOV (25 28 arcsec ^2 ), we note that NGC 1404 has a kinematic decoupled core (KDC) counter-rotating with respect to the external structure of this object. This should explain the sinusoidal behaviour of the 1D profile along the PA For IC 5181, note that the profile along the perpendicular direction is not zero along the region where V_ rad > 0 km s ^ 1 . This is possibly related to the twist detected in the stellar structure in rotation of this galaxy. In the internal region of NGC 7097, it should be noted that there may be both corotating and counter-rotating orbits. The peculiarity of the stellar kinematics of this object is also reflected in the 1D profile along the perpendicular direction of the stellar rotation, whose V _ rad values are not constant along the radius. Velocity dispersion maps The velocity dispersion maps are shown in Fig. cinematica_estelar_1 . Their 1D profiles, shown in Fig. perfil_stellar_kin_1 , were also extracted along and perpendicularly to the directions of the stellar rotations detected in the radial velocity maps. For NGC 1399, NGC 2663, and NGC 3136, where we did not detect any evidence of ordered stellar rotation, the 1D profiles were extracted along the longest and the shortest spatial dimension of the data cubes. They are shown in Fig. fig:perfil_disp_N2663_N1399 . In these three galaxies, both profiles traverse their nuclei. We noticed that the velocity dispersion has higher values in the nuclear region of the galaxies ESO 208 G-21, IC 1459, IC 5181, NGC 4546, and also NGC 1399. The positions of these nuclear peaks of velocity dispersion are given in Table tab_mapa_vel_radial_stellar . We do not see any evidence of peaks in the velocity dispersion maps of NGC 1380, NGC 2663, and NGC 3136. In NGC 1404, we detected a sigma-drop in the central region. This feature has already been reported by 1998A&AS..133..325G . We will revisit the sigma-drop detected in NGC 1404 in Section ngc1404_case . The nuclear peaks detected in ESO 208 G-21, IC 1459, IC 5181, and NGC 4546 should correspond, in part, to unresolved stellar rotation. This interpretation is not new at all (see e.g., 1987IAUS..127...17K,1988ApJ...327L..55F ). First, the peaks of velocity dispersion agree, within the errors, with the positions of the kinematic centre and also with the positions of the AGNs of these four objects. Also, note that in IC 1459, IC 5181 and NGC 4546, the shape of the peaks are elongated perpendicularly to the direction of the rotation. The 1D profiles confirm this statement, since the velocity dispersion decreases slowly along this direction. A possible interpretation for this characteristic is that it is caused by a stellar structure in rotation. Along the plane of rotation, the sigma peak has a width limited by the PSF. On the perpendicular direction it may not be limited by the PSF as the size of the structure may go beyond it. In NGC 7097, the elongated structure detected in the velocity dispersion map is a possible consequence of the complexity of the stellar kinematics discussed in Section stellar_radial_velocities . Also the elongation detected in IC 1459, IC 5181, and NGC 4546 may be the result of a more complex setup of their stellar kinematics. For NGC 1380, one would expect a peaky structure, on the basis of the radial velocity map, somewhat similar to those of ESO 208-G21, IC 5181, IC 1459 or NGC 4546.

Instead, for unknown reasons, it shows an irregular structure with a tendency to increase towards the centre. 2007ApJ...671.1321G , using data from the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) , analysed the stellar kinematics of NGC 1399, where we detected a nuclear peak, but no sign of stellar rotation. They showed that the stellar orbits are predominantly tangential near the SMBH. However, our data do not have enough spatial resolution to detect the decrease of the observed velocity dispersion that results from this anisotropy in the nuclear region. Circumnuclear velocity dispersion of the sample galaxies To obtain a representative value of the circumnuclear velocity dispersion of the sample galaxies, we calculated _ * within a radius R_ * as _ * _ i=1 ^ N_p I_i _i^2+V_i^2 _ i=1 ^ N_p I_i , where I_i is the intensity of the stellar continuum in the spaxel i and N_p is the total number of spaxels within R_ * . The values of _ * and R_ * are shown in Table tab_disp_vel_stellar . The statistical errors for _ * were estimated with a MC simulation and are 1 km s ^ 1 . Maps of the Gauss Hermite h_3 moment The maps of the h_3 moment are shown in Fig. cinematica_estelar_1 . The profiles extracted along and perpendicularly to the directions of the stellar rotations detected in the radial velocity maps are shown in Fig. perfil_stellar_kin_1 . The most remarkable feature of the h_3 maps is the anti-correlation between this parameter and the radial velocities in the galaxies where we detected stellar structures in rotation. LOSVDs with signatures of asymmetry are related to rotating stellar structures embedded in the spheroidal structure, which is common for some E galaxies 1994MNRAS.270..325C,2010gfe..book.....M . These asymmetric profiles have already been detected for IC 1459 by 1988ApJ...327L..55F . Indeed, these authors interpreted this result as a kinematically cold disclike component that is embedded in a kinematically hot structure, although they described the LOSVD profile as a sum of two Gaussian functions. In NGC 1404, the anti-correlation between h_3 and the radial velocity is probably a consequence of the KDC also being embedded in the spheroidal structure of this object. Comparison of the GMOS-IFU results with other data sets Comparison with long-slit data In order to check the consistency of our results, we compared the stellar kinematics extracted from the GMOS data cubes with other data sets presented in the literature. The kinematics of the galaxy IC 1459 were previously analysed by 2002ApJ...578..787C using long-slit data from the HST and from ground-based spectroscopy. In Fig. perfil_comparacao_ic1459 , we show 1D profiles of the radial velocity, velocity dispersion and h_3 moment (only ground-based results) along the major-axis of IC 1459 extracted from the GMOS data cube and from both long-slit spectrographs. One may see that the results from GMOS seem to be consistent with the data presented by 2002ApJ...578..787C . The differences are probably caused by the fact that these measurements have distinct spatial resolutions. For NGC 1399 and NGC 1404, we obtained ground-based long-slit data from 1998A&AS..133..325G along the major-axis of these objects. Fig. perfil_comp_fornax shows the 1D profiles of the kinematic parameters extracted from GMOS and from the long-slit data. Again, one may see a consistency between the GMOS and long-slit measurements. Moreover, the GMOS results of both galaxies match the stellar kinematics IFU data presented by 2014MNRAS.441..274S . A direct comparison between Fig. perfil_comp_fornax and fig. A2 from 2014MNRAS.441..274S confirms this statement. It is worth mentioning that both long-slit data and the IFU data from 2014MNRAS.441..274S do not have enough spatial resolution to resolve the KDC in NGC 1404. In fact, the differences between all results seem to be caused by distinct spatial resolutions among the data sets. Comparison with SAURON data cubes The galaxy NGC 4546 is also part of the SAURON sample and of the ATLAS 3D project. So, we decided to analyse their data cube of this object 2011MNRAS.413..813C The data cube was downloaded from the ATLAS 3D project website at http://www-astro.physics.ox.ac.uk/atlas3d/ mainly to compare the circumnuclear velocity dispersion measurements. We built kinematic maps for this data cube using exactly the same procedures described in Section ppxf_meth . We assured that the kinematic centres of both data cubes have the same radial velocity. In Fig. perfil_comparacao_atlas , we compare the 1D profile of the kinematic maps taken from the GMOS data cubes and from the SAURON data cubes along the direction of the stellar rotations. We used the same PA from Table tab_mapa_vel_radial_stellar for this extraction. Again, the differences that are seen in the 1D profiles are probably caused by the fact that the data sets do not have the same spatial resolution. Indeed, the 1D profile of the velocity dispersion shows that both measurements agree for > 1.0 arcsec, where the effects of the seeing of the GMOS data cube are less pronounced. Also the h_3 results seem to reasonably match each other. We may also compare if the values measured for _ * agree for both data cubes. For the SAURON data cube, we found _ * = 241 12 km s ^ 1 (assuming that the systematic error for the SAURON data cubes is 5 , as stated by 2006MNRAS.366.1126C ), while we found _ * = 254 1 km s ^ 1 using the GMOS data cube. Although both measurements agree within the errors, we explore some possibilities to explain this difference between them: since the spatial sampling of data cubes from the ATLAS 3D project is 0.8 arcsec, we resampled the GMOS data cubes to the same spaxel size. We built kinematic maps using such a configuration and we obtained _ * = 253 1 km s ^ 1 . Also the spectral resolutions of both data cubes are different ( _ inst = 30 km s ^ 1 in = 5577 \ for this GMOS data cube and, from 2004MNRAS.352..721E , _ inst = 108 km s ^ 1 for the SAURON data cube). So, we modified the spectral resolution of the GMOS data cubes to _ inst = 108 km s ^ 1 , but maintaining the spaxels with sizes of 0.2 arcsec. With this configuration, _ * = 243 1 km s ^ 1 . Using spaxels with sizes of 0.8 arcsec and a spectral resolution _ inst = 108 km s ^ 1 for the GMOS data cubes, we calculated _ * = 257 1 km s ^ 1 . Although the values of _ * for the GMOS data cube with spaxels of 0.2 arcsec and _ inst = 108 km s ^ 1 and for the SAURON data cube match each other, the spectral resolution does not seem to be, alone, the cause of the systematic differences for the GMOS data cubes. The point here is that the kinematic maps for the GMOS data cubes with such a configuration is noisier than the same results with any other configuration. Moreover, the GMOS data cube with exactly the same instrumental configuration as the SAURON data cube provides _ * = 257 1 km s ^ 1 , which is almost the same result found for the original GMOS configuration. Another parameter is the seeing of the observation, but it does not seem to affect the measurements made with GMOS. We found _ * = 251 1 km s ^ 1 using the GMOS data cube in its original configuration, but with a FWHM of the PSF degraded to 1.4 arcsec (the original PSF has a FWHM = 0.71 arcsec). One possible source of systematic errors in the _ * measurements using GMOS data cubes is the wavelength range where ppxf is applied. To test this, we fitted each spectrum of the GMOS data cube of NGC 4546 with the same wavelength range as the SAURON data cube, which is from 4800 to 5250 . We found _ * = 243 1 km s ^ 1 . We also applied ppxf from 5250 to 6100 . For this setup, we obtained _ * = 223 1 km s ^ 1 . These differences between the values may come from the fact that GMOS observations made with the R831-G5322 grating is noisier in the blue region of the spectra. Also, the spectral resolution is constant in wavelength but not in velocity. It seems that, depending on the wavelength range, the values for the _ * parameter may vary by 10 , at least for NGC 4546. For IC 1459, for example, which was observed with the B600-G5323 grating (that provides a better response on the blue region of the spectra), we obtained _ * = 319 1 km s ^ 1 for the 4800 \ to 5250 \ range and 322 1 km s ^ 1 for the 5250 \ to 6100 . Both measurements are very close to _ * = 325 1 km s ^ 1 , obtained when we fitted all the wavelength range of the data cube. It is worth mentioning that only these two gratings were used in all sample galaxies (see paper I). A systematic investigation about this issue will be revisited in a future paper, but, for now, we suggest that the errors caused by applying ppxf to different wavelength ranges may be as large as 10 . NGC 1404 was classified as a fast rotator by 2014MNRAS.441..274S . They used an IFU with a spaxel of 1 arcsec ^2 and a FOV of 25 28 arcsec ^2 . By comparing our results with those obtained by 2014MNRAS.441..274S , we conclude that NGC 1404 has a KDC with an extension of 200 pc that is counter-rotating with respect to the external structure of the galaxy. Indeed, the presence of a decoupled structure in NGC 1404 was suggested by 2014MNRAS.441..274S due to the detection of two peaks in their velocity dispersion map. 2008IAUS..245..303G have also reported this counter-rotating KDC. 2006MNRAS.373..906M found KDCs with diameters of about one-hundred pc in some ETGs. These features were detected in fast rotating galaxies and are counter-rotating with respect to their external stellar components. According to these researchers, the host galaxies of these compact KDCs are among the youngest ETGs of the SAURON sample. Moreover, these KDCs are usually (but not always) younger than the overall population of their host galaxies. Although this result might suggest a link between counter-rotation and enhanced star formation, 2006MNRAS.373..906M showed that it is more likely to detect compact KDCs in young galaxies due to the effect of luminosity-weighting. By means of a simple model, they demonstrated that when a KDC of a galaxy gets older, its mass-to-light ratio increases and it becomes harder to distinguish the KDC from the background rotation field. The results of their model revealed that KDCs older than 5 Gyr are barely seen in the radial velocity maps. In other words, it is easier to detect a younger KDC, but they may have a broad range of ages. Indeed, 2006MNRAS.373..906M showed in their fig. 16 that, among six galaxies with KDCs with intrinsic sizes of 100 pc, in five the central luminosity-weighted ages of these objects are between 0.5 and 4 Gyr. Only one galaxy has such a compact structure with a luminosity-weighted age of 18 Gyr. Probably, NGC 1404 would be similar to these objects studied by 2006MNRAS.373..906M , since the estimated age of the central region of NGC 1404 is about 5 8 Gyr 2008IAUS..245..303G . We detected a drop in the velocity dispersion map of NGC 1404. Moreover, the velocity dispersion profile rises more steeply along the direction of the stellar rotations. This may be a consequence of the superposition of the two stellar components in counter-rotation. Indeed, 2007MNRAS.379..418C , using three-integral Schwarzschild models, showed that such a configuration predicts two peaks in the velocity dispersion along the rotation of both stellar components. This result for NGC 1404 resembles that of the 2 galaxies (those objects that shows two off-centred peaks in the velocity dispersion maps along the major axis of the galaxy), as defined by 2011MNRAS.414.2923K , although it is worth mentioning that the definition of such a class of objects given by these authors claims that the separation of the peaks of the maximum values of the velocity dispersion should be, at least, half of the effective radius. However, the data from the ATLAS 3D project, analysed by 2011MNRAS.414.2923K , does not have enough spatial resolution to detect velocity dispersion peaks with a separation of a few arcsec. Thus, a systematic study with seeing-limited data would be of great value in order to better understand objects with the same characteristics. Another hypothesis is that the drop is not related to the KDC. In this case, we may speculate the existence a star-forming region that is kinematically cold and dominates the stellar light within a projected radius smaller than the PSF of the data cube of this object ( 1 arcsec, see paper I). According to 2006MNRAS.369..853W , kinematically cold gas is able to form stars in the centre of barred late-type galaxies. They showed that a star formation rate of 1 M _ yr^ 1 is enough to maintain kinematically cold stars, and thus a drop. The reason is that, although there are mechanisms that make stars kinematically hot (e.g., gravitational disturbances), the nuclear region is fueled again with kinematically cold stars. However, NGC 1404 is a non-barred ETG. One possible scenario suggested by 2006MNRAS.369..853W is that the star formation rate is quite low, possibly undetectable. In this case, the drop may fade away, but over a long time-scale ( 100 Myr). Therefore, an in-depth study of the stellar populations of NGC 1404 is necessary to verify this hypothesis ( 2008IAUS..245..303G do not give much detail of their analysis of the stellar populations of NGC 1404). In addition, a detailed modelling of the stellar kinematics would be interesting, since it may confirm if this is either related to a kinematically cold component or associated with the KDC. Stellar kinematics may provide information about the formation history of galaxies. Fast and slow rotators, for example, have different formation paths 2014MNRAS.444.3357N . However, to assert a global characteristic for the stellar kinematics, data cubes with a FOV that englobes, at least, the region within one effective radius of the galaxies are necessary. The problem is that such data cubes have a spatial resolution that is limited by the size of the fibre (diameters of 2 arcsec). Observations using seeing-limited IFU data are, thus, very important in order to access information about the circumnuclear (scales of 100 pc) properties of the stellar kinematics. There are features that are only seen in such scales, as in the case of NGC 1404 [ but see also 2006MNRAS.373..906M ] . The point here is that an analysis with seeing-limited data cubes may provide complementary information about the stellar kinematics of a galaxy as a whole. Another interesting issue is that, with such data cubes, one may look for off-centred AGNs in a reliable way. Comparing the position of the kinematic centre with the position of the AGNs and also with the position of the photometric centre of the stellar structure may reveal that the SMBH is not always at the centre of a galaxy (see e.g. 2014ApJ...796L..13M ). Thus, the main subject of the present paper is to provide stellar kinematics results from seeing-limited data cubes for the sample galaxies. A more complete analysis in order to search for general characteristics of the stellar kinematics from the circumnuclear region of massive ETGs demands more data and is not possible with the present sample. Below we present the main findings of the present paper. We detected stellar structures in rotation embedded in a kinematically hot stellar structure (spheroid) in the circumnuclear region of seven galaxies of the sample. The other three objects do not seem to possess any sign of stellar rotation within a radius of 100 pc from the nucleus. The galaxy IC 5181 seems to have a small variation of the PA of the stellar structure in rotation with radius. This may be a consequence of a non-axisymmetric potential within the central region of the galaxy. It is worth mentioning that an evidence of a non-axisymmetric potential for this galaxy is also seen in the gas kinematics (paper III). In six galaxies, we detected a nuclear peak in the velocity dispersion maps. A fraction of this peak in four galaxies is likely to be associated with the stellar rotation in a region that is not fully resolved. In NGC 1380, NGC 2663, NGC 3136, and NGC 7097, the velocity dispersion maps are more complex and require a careful analysis for a reliable interpretation.

sources.summary: FCC 167 is described as the brightest Fornax lenticular inside the virial radius, with an extended rotationally supported disc, high central dispersion, faint H emission, dust, and stellar-population studies.

quotes.quote: FCC 167 (NGC 1380) -- Fig. fig:FCC167map FCC 167 is the brightest lenticular galaxy inside the virial radius of the Fornax cluster at R_ proj =0.6^ from the centre. It is an ancient infaller in the north-south clump of galaxies on the western side of the cluster. This galaxy shows an extended rotationally-supported stellar disc with a large rotation velocity ( v_ max 300 km s ^ 1 ) and a high velocity dispersion in the centre ( 200 km s ^ 1 ), where there is also faint H emission indicative of traces of star formation. Sarzi2018 chose FCC 167 as an illustrative example to describe the details of the F3D data reduction and analysis. Viaene2019 studied the dust and ionised-gas properties in the centre of FCC 167, and MartinNavarro2019 provided a detailed investigation of its stellar population properties and initial mass function.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC1380 as an unbarred lenticular whose blue and red GC populations differ, with large isophotal overdensities and a complex inner red-GC structure.

quotes.quote: NGC1380 is an unbarred, lenticular galaxy whose GCS, investigated both from the ground kisslerpatig1997 and using HST data chiessantos2007 , features two clearly distinct color classes: the blue GCs, which are spherically distributed and mostly located in the outskirts of the galaxy, and the red GCs that follow more closely the geometry of the photometric components of the disk and bulge. Three major overdensities are observed in the residual map generated by all ACSFCS GCs in NGC1380 (Figure fig:ngc1380 ), along the D _ 25 elliptical isophote: C1 is located in the E side of the galaxy, C2 and C4 are located in the W side. While C1 is only barely visible in the blue residual map, C2 and C4 are clearly detected in both the blue and red GCs, although in the red only one overdensity region engulfing both structure is observed. The blue residual map suggests that the morphology of C2 and C4 is elongated and aligned along radial directions towards the center of the galaxies. C3 is a morphologically complex structure with multiple peak densities and located at a small galactocentric distance NE of the galaxy center; C3 is only visible in the red GCs.

sources.summary: ALMA CO modeling of NGC 1380 finds a rotating circumnuclear disk and a central black-hole mass near 1.47e8 solar masses, limited by dust and stellar-mass degeneracies.

quotes.quote: NGC 1380 is classified as an SA0 galaxy in both the Third Reference Catalogue of Bright Galaxies (RC3; 1991rc3..book.....D ) and in the Hyperleda database 2003A&A...412...45P .

For NGC 1380, a slight central increase is observed in its maximum LOS velocity, reaching approximately 300 km s^ 1 relative to the systemic velocity of the galaxy, as expected of rotation around a central BH.

For NGC 1380, we determine = 1.47 10^8 M_ with an uncertainty of about 40 by optimizing thin disk models to the ALMA observations with four different host galaxy models. We find that our measured values of are degenerate with the enclosed stellar mass, and that the uncertainties associated with the dust corrections and host galaxy models dominate the error budget. Given the slight central rise in observed LOS velocity, it is possible that higher resolution ALMA observations could provide a more confident determination of the BH mass by lifting the stellar and BH mass degeneracy.

sources.summary: The paper observes NGC 1380, identifies it as a Type Ia supernova host, shows its NIRCam core and dust lane, and derives a TRGB distance modulus.

quotes.quote: In this paper we report on observations of three galaxies in the cluster: NGC 1404 with a prior HST TRGB measurement and host to two Type Ia supernovae 2021ApJ...915...34H,2022ApJ...932...15A , NGC 1380 that hosted a single Type Ia supernova 1993ApJ...415..589K , and NGC 1399, the central dominant galaxy in the cluster.

To further highlight the dynamic range probed by NIRCam in a single pointing, we show color ( F090W+F150W+F356W ) images of individual chips from the NGC 1380 visit in Fig. fig:n1380color . The core of this galaxy is centered on the B4 chip; a small dust lane in the center is readily apparent. While individual stars cannot be resolved in the inner regions due to crowding, a few globular clusters are visible scattered throughout.

For our final distance moduli, we take the average of each of the two individual fields (without further averaging down of the errors), to find = 31.397 0.066 mag for NGC 1380, = 31.511 0.068 mag for NGC 1399, and = 31.364 0.067 mag for NGC 1404 (Table tab:qual-distances ).

sources.summary: The paper discusses FCC 167 as a possible endpoint of diffuse intracluster light connected to FCC 213, noting similar SBF distances and a 250 kpc projected separation.

quotes.quote: The diffuse light is clearly significant from FCC 213 to FCC 184 and FCC 167, but only marginally significant north of FCC 170. The future EWS data, in which a correction for straylight in will be implemented that is not yet available, will be needed to check whether or not diffuse light really extends from the central regions of the galaxy cluster to FCC 167 in the north of our FoV, or to FCC 147 towards the west. Such extensions are not seen in the ground-based data of Iodice-2017 , but a connection to FCC 167 would not be too surprising considering the current understanding of the three-dimensional structure of this area of the Fornax cluster. Indeed, according to the surface brightness fluctuation (SBF) estimates of Blakeslee2009 , recalled in Table gal-catalogue-m , FCC 213 and FCC 167 are at similar distances, respectively, (20.9 0.9) Mpc and (21.2 0.7) Mpc . The projected separation between these two galaxies is only about 250 kpc .

sources.summary: The paper finds that NGC 1380's new kinematic data agree with previous observations but reveal localized substructure, central enhanced dispersion, and evidence for a distinct kinematic component.

quotes.quote: NGC 1380 Figure fig:comp1 shows that the new data agree extremely well with the previous observations, but with much improved error bounds. The new data with their smaller errors and unfolded presentation give some indication of localized substructure that differs between the two sides of the galaxy. An enhancement in velocity dispersion within the central 7 arcsec , first suggested by D'Onofrio et al. \ (1995), is strongly confirmed with the new data. It is also notable from Figure fig:1380dat that this region also shows structure in the h_3 skewness measure, indicative of a distinct kinematic component. Interestingly, this feature coincides with photometric evidence for a distinct component which comprises a strong variation in isophotal ellipticity and position angle (Caon et al. 1994).

sources.summary: The paper uses HST/WFPC2 photometry to study NGC 1380's star-cluster system, finding about 570 candidates, three color/size peaks, and extended clusters unlike Galactic globular clusters.

quotes.quote: We have analysed deep WFPC2/HST images of NGC 1380 in the B and V filters, from which a sample of about 570 star cluster candidates was drawn. The colour distribution contains three distinct peaks, two of which have been observed in previous works form the ground (Kissler-Patig et al. ). In order to better characterise these cluster subpopulations we attempted to measure sizes. We also identify three distinct peaks in the resulting size distribution, the main of them being similar to typical globular clusters, whereas the other two corresponding to more extended clusters. We compare both the luminosities and sizes of our sample to the Galactic counterparts: the extended NGC 1380 clusters are not well fit by the Galactic clusters size distribution; the extended clusters also tend to be less luminous than typical GCs in the Galaxy.

sources.summary: NGC 1380 hosts SN 1992A and has a deeply observed globular-cluster system split into metal-rich bulge and metal-poor halo populations for GCLF calibration.

quotes.quote: NGC 1380, host galaxy for SN 1992A As already mentioned, SN 1992A is one of the best ever observed Ia SNe and therefore enters the zero point determination of the Ia Hubble diagram with a high weight. The GCS of NGC 1380 has been analysed by Kissler-Patig et al. and Della Valle et al. . Interestingly, it turned out that the GCS could be separated into an elongated metal-rich bulge and a spherical, metal-poor halo population. Due to the excellent data quality (very long exposed NTT data in very good seeing conditions) it was possible to trace the luminosity function 1 mag below the TOM, and thus the GCS of NGC 1380 is the deepest observed GCS from ground-based data. Table tab:92A lists the apparent brightnesses and the widths of the t_5 functions for B , V , and I .

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 1380 is a bright S0 that follows elliptical-galaxy index relations, shows strong Mg indices relative to Fe, and has an Mg-overabundant nucleus.

quotes.quote: First, faint S0s have a large scatter with respect to the main relations, while the bright galaxies NGC 1380 and NGC 1381 closely follow the relations for ellipticals estimated by K00 (red dashed lines in Figure fig:indsig ).

What is clear from almost all these panels is that the three highest-dispersion galaxies in the sample (open symbols in Figure fig:FevsMg ) are not accurately described by models that assume solar abundance ratios, presenting stronger Mg indices for a given Fe index than the model predictions. Among these three galaxies, the effect seems to be stronger for NGC 1380 and 1381, while NGC 1316 (the merger remnant) is usually closer to the solar-model grids. In similar plots, K00 found that only the nucleus of NGC 1380 presented this behaviour, suggesting that this galaxy is Mg overabundant compared to Fe.

sources.summary: NGC 1380 is a fast-rotating S0 with high central velocity dispersion, constant age, nonmonotonic metallicity, and central magnesium over-abundance.

quotes.quote: NGC 1380 = FCC 167 This is a fast rotating S0 galaxy, ( V_ max 210 ), with a central velocity dispersion of 230 . The age is constant along the radius. The metallicity first decreases by 0.2 dex (up to 1 ) and after increases. We notice an Mg over-abundance in the centre.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 1380 as an early-type host galaxy in its globular-cluster spectroscopy sample and reports a clearly bimodal globular-cluster colour distribution.

quotes.quote: The host galaxies (NGC 1380, NGC 2434, NGC 3115, NGC 3379, NGC 3585, NGC 5846, and NGC 7192) were selected to sample a significant range in environmental density at intermediate galaxy luminosity and velocity dispersion in the range 19.2 M_B 21.2 and 184 264 km s ^ 1 , respectively. All galaxies are of early type ( T<-2 ) according to the RC3 galaxy catalog RC3 . Our sample includes five elliptical and two lenticular galaxies (see Tab. tab:galdat ).
We find clear signs for bi-modality in the globular cluster colour distributions of NGC 1380, 3115, and 3585.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1380 as an S0/a Fornax galaxy with central dust, a kinematically decoupled core, and an optically thick central dust lane.

quotes.quote: Three galaxies (NGC 1316, NGC 1344, and NGC 1380 -- see details below) have clear central dust features.
NGC 1380 - contains a kinematically decoupled core (KDC) lon94 . It also contains an optically thick, well-organized central dust lane, easily visible in available HST images.

sources.summary: The paper notes NGC 1380's telluric-correction issue, central dust features, and clear dust lane with significant IRAS dust mass, while its near-infrared velocity dispersion is lower than the optical value.

quotes.quote: For NGC 1380, telluric lines have not been removed, because of a problem with the wavelength calibration of the standard star.
Correlation with IRAS dust masses According to , at least two galaxies (NGC 1316 and NGC 1380) from our sample show clear central dust features.

A clear example is the case of NGC 1380: in spite of a clear dust lane in optical images Jordan2007 and a significant IRAS dust mass of 7.6 10^5 M_ , the NIR velocity dispersion is lower than the optical dispersion.

gmw_00003382.name: 3C254
gmw_00003382.overview: The quasar 3C254 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus that exhibits a highly asymmetric radio structure, featuring one lobe positioned very close to its core [arXiv:1211.5778]. It is situated within a luminous cluster of galaxies and displays extended X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/9907034].

sources.summary: The quasar 3C 254 shows extended X-ray emission consistent with a luminous cluster environment, with PSPC residuals and HRI structure not explained by a secondary source.

quotes.quote: 3C 254 The radial profile of the PSPC observation of this quasar is poorly fit by a point-source model, with positive residuals on scales between 30 and 60 arcsec. The HRI observation detects a weak secondary source 30 arcsec to the E, unidentified on Digitized Sky Survey (DSS) Available on the World Wide Web at URL: < http://stdatu.stsci.edu/dss/ > plates, and too close to the quasar to be resolved by the PSPC. The secondary source is not responsible for all of the extension seen in either the HRI or PSPC data; it may, in any case, be a clump in extended thermal emission. There is optical evidence that 3C 254 lies in a cluster of galaxies (Bremer 1997), and there is no evidence in the HRI images for X-ray emission from the foreground galaxy to the NE of the quasar reported by Bremer (1997). We fit to the radial profile of the PSPC observations, where there are more counts and so better statistics; dewobbling has no effect on the results.

The counts in the extended component indicate a luminous cluster; no cooling flow is required unless kT 2.5 keV, but we cannot rule out a contribution to the point-source component from unresolved cooling gas. If we assume kT = 7.9 keV, then the rest-frame 0.3--3.0 keV luminosity of the extended component is 10^ 38 W, consistent with the estimate of Crawford Vanderreist (1997) who examine the spectrum of the same PSPC data.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C254 as an asymmetric radio quasar whose close lobe blends with the core in FIRST, causing a severe core-dominance misclassification.

quotes.quote: Of the four triple and lobe sources, the derived R values for three are in very close agreement. The fourth, B1111+408=3C254, is very discrepant; for this source, _ 10 R = 1.8 according to Jackson et al., but Table 2 of K11a lists this source as having a core flux density of 2004 mJy and an extended flux density of 1030 mJy. Examination of high-resolution radio maps reveals that the former is correct, and that the origin of the discrepancy is that this source has a very asymmetric structure, with one lobe close to the core (Owen Puschell 1984; Saikia et al., 1990). On the lower resolution FIRST images, this lobe appears as part of the core, leading to an overestimate by a factor of 100 of the R value, thus incorrectly leading to its classification as a core-dominated radio source.

sources.summary: The source is piled up, but its power-law slope, flux, and luminosity agree with earlier Chandra analysis, while its 1-keV flux exceeds the ROSAT value.

quotes.quote: 3C 254 : the source is piled up. Within uncertainties our power-low slope is in agreement with that found by donahue03 using the same data. The flux and luminosity are also in agreement after considering the different cosmological parameters. The 1-keV flux density is 1/3 greater than that found by mjh99 , but this is likely to be within the large uncertainties arising from the ROSAT analysis.

gmw_00003383.name: UCM0537--0227
gmw_00003383.overview: UCM0537--0227 is a background emission-line galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of approximately 0.1009 [arXiv:0809.0996]. It is a star-forming galaxy that exhibits an extended appearance, red infrared colors, strong PAH features, and a moderate star formation rate [arXiv:0809.0996].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UCM0537--0227 as a background emission-line, star-forming galaxy with red infrared colors, extended appearance, z=0.1009, PAH features, and moderate star formation.

quotes.quote: UCM0537--0227 was the faintest new Orionis photometric member candidate in the Mayrit catalogue of Caballero (2008c). He gave the DENIS i and 2MASS JHK_ s magnitudes for the object, from where one could derive a mass in the middle of the brown dwarf domain, if it belonged to the cluster. He also noticed that UCM0537--0227 possibly has an extended, non-stellar point spread function. Its extremely red 2MASS ( J-K_ s = 2.34 0.11 mag) and IRAC/ Spitzer colours measured by Caballero et al. (2008) made us to obtain spectroscopy of it. The low-resolution optical spectrum (top panel of Fig. spHandI ) and the spectral energy distribution of UCM0537--0227 correspond to an emission-line, star-forming galaxy at a spectroscopic redshift z _ sp = 0.1009 0.0002 with strong PAH features and a moderate star formation rate.

Two of the targets in our sample were identified as low redshift (z 0.10--0.24) galaxies with prominent emission-lines in the optical and large fluxes at 8--24 m due to an active galactic nucleus (UCM0536--0239) and a burst of star formation (UCM0537--0227).

gmw_00003384.name: UCM0536--0239
gmw_00003384.overview: UCM0536--0239 is a low-redshift Type 1 obscured quasi-stellar object located at a redshift of approximately 0.2362 [arXiv:0809.0996]. It is an active galactic nucleus that exhibits X-ray emission and an extended optical morphology [arXiv:0809.0996].

sources.summary: The paper classifies UCM0536--0239 as a low-redshift Type 1 obscured quasi-stellar object with odd infrared colors, X-ray emission, extended morphology, and AGN-dominated mid-infrared flux.

quotes.quote: UCM0536--0239 ( [ HHM2007 ] 668) and its odd spectral energy distribution from the I band to [ 8.0 ] was first identified by Caballero (2006). Just afterwards, Hern andez et al. (2007) measured its [ 24 ] band magnitude and presented it as one of the very few class I object candidates in Orionis. Caballero (2007b) noticed that UCM0536--0239 (incorrectly named ``Mayrit 111208'' in his work) had quite blue I-J and extremely red J-K_ s colours for its faint magnitude ( J = 16.14 0.10 mag) and found it to be an X-ray emitter detected with the ACIS and HRC-I instruments onboard Chandra .

The (sub)stellar nature of UCM0536--0239 was put into question by Caballero (2008c): he measured a possible extended point spread function in public optical images. Finally, Skinner et al. (2008) measured the mean photon energy of UCM0536--0239 at E = 3.40 keV from ACIS/ Chandra data, which is a quite high value for a normal star, but typical of active galactic nuclei. The ALFOSC spectrum (bottom panel of Fig. spHandI ) and the spectral energy distribution of UCM0536--0239 favour its classification as a Type 1 obscured quasi-stellar object at z _ sp = 0.2362 0.0005 .

gmw_00003385.name: A2146-A
gmw_00003385.overview: A2146-A is a bright radio galaxy [arXiv:1811.09708] and the brightest cluster galaxy of the southeastern subcluster in Abell 2146 [arXiv:1508.01505, arXiv:1811.09708]. It is an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1508.01505] that unexpectedly lags behind the X-ray cool core and bow shock front [arXiv:1508.01505, arXiv:1708.03641].

sources.summary: The paper highlights the A2146-A BCG as offset from the X-ray cool core and spectroscopically complex, with absorption plus strong emission lines and discrepant absorption/emission velocity estimates.

quotes.quote: The Brightest Cluster Galaxy (hereafter BCG) of the SE subcluster (A2146-A), is in an unexpected location lagging behind the X-ray cool core canning . As noted above, in the early stages of a cluster merger the galaxies should be nearly collisionless and follow the dark matter distribution, which should lead the collisional X-ray gas (e.g. clowe, markevitch ). In addition to the strange projected location of the BCG in Abell 2146-A, the system is unusual in that despite it appearing to undergo a major galaxy cluster merger it does not have a detected radio halo russell11 .

The spectrum of the BCG in A2146-A, however, shows many emission lines. The three most prominent features are H_ and two [ OIII ] lines. Zooming in on this spectrum (Fig. zoom ) shows the characteristic absorption spectrum expected for an elliptical galaxy with additional strong emission features. While most of the absorption features are blended with emission features, making it difficult to accurately determine redshift, the Na D (rest wavelength of 5895 ) feature is relatively unblended and yields a velocity of 69,612 km s ^ 1 . This is a lower redshift than determined through cross correlation methods for the BCG which rely on the emission lines (69,897 km s ^ 1 ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies A2146-A as a bright radio galaxy and SE brightest cluster galaxy, measuring its 144 MHz flux, average spectral index, and high-frequency spectral steepening.

quotes.quote: A2146 is known to host two bright radio galaxies (namely A2146-A and A2146-B) that are located in the central regions of the NW and SW sub-clusters (see Fig. fig:hlres , left). One of these is the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) belonging to the SE sub-cluster e.g. Russell2011 . In projection, the radio galaxies are approximately coincident with the extended non-thermal emission from the ICM Hlavacek-Larrondo2017 and are situated behind the SE and NW merger shocks e.g. Russell2011 . The total flux density of these sources at 144 MHz is 75 11 mJy for A2146-A (i.e. the SE BCG) and 166 25 mJy for A2146-B.

We find that the average spectral index is 0.71 0.02 for A2146-A and 0.84 0.02 for A2146-B. However, the spectral energy distribution in Fig. fig:int_ps hints at possible spectral breaks at about 612 MHz and 1.5 GHz for A2146-A and A2146-B, respectively. To quantify this, we fit the radio flux density of the galaxies with a double power-law function. The resulting spectral indices are _ 144 MHz ^ 612 MHz =-0.53 0.11 and _ 612 MHz ^ 5 GHz =-0.79 0.05 for A2146-A and _ 144 MHz ^ 1.5 GHz =-0.64 0.06 and _ 1.5 GHz ^ 5 GHz =-1.17 0.10 for A2146-B, where in both cases the spectral index steepens at high frequencies.

sources.summary: The paper describes A2146-A as Abell 2146's brightest cluster galaxy, unexpectedly lagging behind the bow-shock gas peak, with one detected radio source coincident with it.

quotes.quote: A2146 also hosts a brightest cluster galaxy (BCG; labelled A2146-A in Fig. fig1 ) which is located 10 arcsec ( 40 kpc) behind the X-ray surface brightness peak, placing it behind the bow shock front. This is unexpected because cluster galaxies should be collisionless, as opposed to the hot intracluster gas which is slowed down by friction e.g. Mar2001563 . In a merger, the galaxies are therefore expected to preceed the hot X-ray gas. This is not the case in A2146, and the location of the BCG is unlikely due to projection effects since the observations of shock fronts require the merger axis to be close to the plane of the sky see also Whi2015453 . Instead, Canning et al. (2012) and White et al. (2015) Can2012420,Whi2015453 argue that it may be due to another galaxy perturbing the system, or that the merger is slightly off-axis.
The only two radio sources detected coincided with A2146-A and a second cluster galaxy to the north (labelled as radio source in Fig. fig1 , see bottom-right panel).

gmw_00003386.name: Cetus II
gmw_00003386.overview: Cetus II is a candidate ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:1712.04597]. It is considered a Local Group satellite [arXiv:2007.05011], though it currently lacks stellar kinematic measurements [arXiv:1712.04597].

sources.summary: The paper derives a first but less robust systemic proper motion for Cetus 2 using looser Gaia cuts because the satellite fraction peak becomes resolved.

quotes.quote: The estimates for Cetus 2, Pictor 2 and Leo T are derived using looser cuts on the Gaia data in order to identify more member stars, and these systems are also discussed in more detail in Section atthelimits .

For Leo T and Cetus 2, the peak in f_ sat is clearly resolved with the looser cuts on the data. For Pictor 2, the situation is more ambiguous, but even here it appears that the favored model has f_ sat > 0 .

We present the measurements for Leo T, Cetus 2 and Pictor 2 using the looser cuts on the data alongside the other measurements in Tables pmresults and preferred . We stress that the proper motions for Leo T, Cetus 2 and Pictor 2 are based in part on photometry which may not be reliable. Any analyses that use these three measurements should proceed with caution, and we expect that future data releases from Gaia - potentially including the imminent EDR3 - may contain sufficently improved photometry to improve these three measurements.

sources.summary: The paper forecasts Cetus II as an ultra-faint dSph candidate without stellar kinematics, assigning predicted dispersions and showing potential sterile-neutrino constraints if it has a large dark-matter distribution.

quotes.quote: In Table tb:Q_value we also show predictions of two dSph candidates that do not yet have associated stellar kinematic measurements: Cetus II and Tucana V. We list predictions for Q and values if they have _* = 3.0 1.0 km/s.

If we extrapolate this relationship, the predicted stellar velocity dispersion values for Cetus II and Tucana V are about 3-4 km/s, which is comparable to other ultra-faint dSphs with similar r_h such as Segue 1 and Draco II.

We also show forecasts for the limits that can be obtained if two compact dSph candidates (Cetus II in green dash dot line; Tucana V in purple dash line) are confirmed to possess large DM distributions with _* = 3.0 1.0 km/s. Tucana V can exclude the parameter space of m_ _s 3.0--4.9 keV for mixing angle sin^2 2 _V 5.2 10^ 9 -7.4 10^ 12 . For Cetus II it can exclude a parameter space of m_ _s 2.3--3.6 keV for mixing angle sin^2 2 _V 9.0 10^ 9 -2.1 10^ 11 .

gmw_00003387.name: Tucana V
gmw_00003387.overview: Tucana V is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2403.13060] with a small stellar mass of approximately 500 solar masses [arXiv:1605.04820]. Its stellar population exhibits a broad metallicity range [arXiv:2403.13060] and lacks mass segregation [arXiv:2112.04689], indicating an inhomogeneous chemical enrichment history [arXiv:2403.13060].

sources.summary: The paper confirms Tuc V as an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy with chemically diverse member stars, a broad metallicity range, and inhomogeneous chemical enrichment history.

quotes.quote: This paper presents an analysis of three stars in the Tucana V system, one in the inner region and two at 10 \ (7--10 half-light radii) from the center. We find a remarkable chemical diversity between the stars. One star exhibits enhancements in rapid neutron-capture elements (an I star), and another is highly enhanced in C, N, and O but with low neutron-capture abundances (a CEMP-no star). The metallicities of the stars analyzed span more than 1 dex from [ Fe/H ] =-3.55 to 2.46 .

This, combined with a large abundance range of other elements like Ca, Sc, and Ni, confirms that Tuc V is an ultra-faint dwarf (UFD) galaxy. The variation in abundances, highlighted by [ Mg/Ca ] ratios ranging from +0.89 to 0.75 , among the stars, demonstrates that the chemical enrichment history of Tuc V was very inhomogeneous. Tuc V is only the second UFD galaxy in which stars located at large distances from the galactic center have been analyzed, along with Tucana II.

sources.summary: The paper treats Tucana V as an ultra-faint dSph candidate without spectroscopy, notes possible SMC-halo or dissolving-cluster interpretations, and forecasts strong sterile-neutrino constraints if dark-matter dominated.

quotes.quote: Here Cetus II and Tucana V are ultra-faint candidates that have no spectrascopic follow-up yet. Among these dSphs, other than Coma Berenices, Segue 1, and Pegasus III Kim_etal15b that are found using Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) data, the rest are found recently from the Dark Energy Survey (DES) Bechtol_etal15, Drlica_Wagner_etal15,Koposov_etal15b (Horologium I, Reticulum II, Cetus II, and Tucana V) and Pan-STARRS Laevens_etal15 (Draco II) data. However, we note that had acquired deep Gemini/GMOS-S to study three ultra-faint dwarf galaxy candidates including Tucana V. They argue that Tucana V has low-level excess of stars in their data without a well-defined centre, and it is likely either a chance grouping of stars related to the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) halo or a star cluster in an advanced stage of dissolution.

We also show forecasts for the limits that can be obtained if two compact dSph candidates (Cetus II in green dash dot line; Tucana V in purple dash line) are confirmed to possess large DM distributions with _* = 3.0 1.0 km/s. Tucana V can exclude the parameter space of m_ _s 3.0--4.9 keV for mixing angle sin^2 2 _V 5.2 10^ 9 -7.4 10^ 12 .

sources.summary: The paper presents Tuc V as a recently discovered ultra-faint dwarf system with 17 pc half-light radius and about 500 solar masses in stars, likely slightly beyond the dynamical-friction stability limit.

quotes.quote: The recently discovered Tuc V and Cet II reported by Drlica-Wagner et al. (2015) are even smaller, with half-light radii of 17 pc in both cases. Although no kinematic studies have been published for either, the position of Tuc V on the magnitude-size plane in the region occupied by other well studied dSphs, together with the ellipticity observed, leads the authors to strongly identify the object as a galaxy. Its small stellar mass of only 500 M_ and known scalings for the ultra faint dSphs, suggest an internal velocity dispersion <3km s^ 1 , which would result in _ DF =8 Gyr , for the central value of its inferred r_ h . Given the inferred age of 10.9 Gyr for Tuc V (Drlica-Wagner et al. 2015), this system is probably slightly beyond the instability limit for dynamical friction shrivelling.

sources.summary: The paper concludes Tucana V is a dwarf galaxy because spectroscopy and photometry were inconclusive, but the authors find no significant mass segregation where a star cluster should be strongly segregated.

quotes.quote: simonetal2020 obtained Magellan/IMACS spectroscopy for 3 stars in Tucana V but found neither a significant metallicity spread nor a velocity dispersion of the system. The photometry by connetal2018 was equally inconclusive since they found that Tucana V is compatible with the location of dwarf galaxies in a luminosity-metallicity plane, but compatible with a star cluster in a size-luminosity plane. We find no significant mass segregation for Tucana V ( P_ Mseg =1.3 ) while as star cluster it should be strongly mass segregated and therefore conclude that Tucana V is a dwarf galaxy.

gmw_00003388.name: CSO 21
gmw_00003388.overview: CSO 21 is a quasar with an initial measured brightness of approximately 17.02 magnitudes in the V band [arXiv:1004.1199]. Detected brightness variations in the object can be modeled by a changing spectral index and a decrease in amplitude [arXiv:1004.1199].

sources.summary: For CSO 21, the paper measures the initial brightness, models the detected variation with a changing spectral index and amplitude, and finds the observations satisfy its simultaneity criterion.

quotes.quote: CSO 21. For this object, we measure a brightness of m_V=17.02 0.03 at the beginning of the observations, which is similar to the value reported by Veron01 , m_V=17.0 . This first set of data is fitted with _ V t_0 = -0.67 0.11 (Figure fig86a ). The variation detected can be explained by an increase of 0.002 of the spectral index and a decrease in amplitude of 4 (Figure fig86c ). It is possible that the OM detected for the V and R bands is also present in the B band, but the variation in this band was not detected due to observational errors. The simultaneity criterion indicates that the level of brightness had not changed during observations of a BVR sequence ( S_B=0.07 , S_V=0.35 , and S_R=0.11 ).

gmw_00003389.overview: 1628.5+3808 is a radio-quiet quasar with an observed magnitude of approximately 16.78 [arXiv:1004.1199]. The spectral variation of this quasar is more accurately reproduced by a thermal component variation than by non-thermal changes [arXiv:1004.1199].

sources.summary: The paper describes 1628.5+3808 as a quasar whose spectral variation is poorly reproduced by non-thermal changes but more accurately by a thermal component.

quotes.quote: 1628.5+3808. The magnitude of this quasar was m_V=16.78 0.02 at the beginning of the observations, i.e., similar to the reported brightness m_V=16.80 by Veron01 . A single non-thermal component fits these data, with _ t_0 = -1.75 0.02 (Figure fig87a ). However, the spectral variation cannot be reproduced by changes in this component (Figures fig87c ).

Then, a thermal component is added to model the data with T_ t_0 10,000 K and a_ V t_0 65 (Figure fig87b ). A variation of non-thermal origin can explain marginally the spectral evolution. On the contrary, a thermal variation can reproduce more accurately the observed behavior (Figure fig87d ).

gmw_00003390.name: US 3472
gmw_00003390.overview: US 3472 is a radio-quiet quasar that has been observed at a brightness magnitude of 16.25 [arXiv:1004.1199]. Its observed color variations cannot be explained by a power-law fit, but they can be reproduced by a thermal variation within a mixed blackbody and non-thermal model [arXiv:1004.1199].

sources.summary: For US 3472, the paper finds the observed color variation cannot be explained by the initial power-law fit, while a thermal variation in a mixed model can reproduce it.

quotes.quote: US 3472. When this object was observed, its brightness was m_V=16.25 0.01 , i.e., brighter than that reported by Lafranca92 and Veron01 ( m_V=16.99 ). Initial data were fitted by a power law with _ t_0 = 0.48 0.01 (Figure fig88a ). Nevertheless, with this arrangement we cannot give an explanation for the color variation shown in the Figure fig88c . The mixture of a blackbody with T_ t_0 27,900 K and a non-thermal component with a_ V t_0 29 can also fit the data (Figure fig88b ). While a change of the non-thermal component cannot explain the observed variation, a thermal variation can do it (Figure fig88d ).

gmw_00003391.name: EGSIRAC J141912.03+524924.0
gmw_00003391.overview: EGSIRAC J141912.03+524924.0 is a gas-rich main-sequence galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 3.2 [arXiv:1902.10143]. It has a high molecular gas fraction of 0.9 and exhibits a star formation efficiency similar to other high-redshift main-sequence galaxies [arXiv:1902.10143].

sources.summary: The paper presents EGSIRAC J141912.03+524924.0 as a gas-rich z=3.2 main-sequence galaxy whose CO emission, excitation, star-formation efficiency, and gas fraction are measured.

quotes.quote: We here present observations of CO( 5-4 ) emission in EGSIRAC J141912.03+524924.0 (hereafter EGS141912), a gas-rich MS galaxy at z 3.2 , detected serendipitously in CO( 3-2 ) emission hereafter ag2017 ag2017 . Our new observations confirm the target redshift and provide some of the first constraints on the molecular gas excitation and star formation efficiency in z > 3 MS galaxies.

We have presented molecular gas observations of EGS141912, one of the highest redshift unlensed MS galaxies detected in CO to date. Our observations of the CO( 3-2 ) and CO( 5-4 ) emission reveal that the gas excitation is consistent with that seen in z 1.5 BzK galaxies, although toward the low end. EGS141912 also has a similar star formation efficiency as other high- z MS galaxies between z 1.5-2.5 . We find EGS141912 to be gas-rich, with a gas fraction of f_ gas 0.9 0.2 , which is consistent with scaling relations for the gas fraction of MS galaxies derived using dust-based measurements of the total ISM mass scoville2017 . The uncertainties on the star formation efficiency and gas fraction for EGS141912 are driven by those on , L_ IR and the unknown gas excitation, and we need both high spatial resolution observations of the CO( 1-0 ) emission as well as observations at the peak of the far-IR SED to improve our knowledge of the cold molecular gas, the molecular gas fraction and its star-formation efficiency.

gmw_00003392.name: Was 45
gmw_00003392.overview: Was 45 is an active galactic nucleus and a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:2403.13194]. X-ray observations of the galaxy favor the presence of at least two warm absorbers [arXiv:2403.13194].

sources.summary: The paper finds Was 45 favors at least two warm absorbers, while noting unmodeled spectral features and deferring a more detailed analysis.

quotes.quote: Was 45 Our results favour the presence of at least 2 warm absorbers. However, there are features in the spectra that are not accounted for by our model. A more detailed analysis will be reported in Gudi no et al. in prep.

gmw_00003393.name: IRAS 15091-2107
gmw_00003393.overview: IRAS 15091-2107 is a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:1106.3023] with a radio-bright compact core [arXiv:2006.01700] and a warm absorber [arXiv:2403.13194]. Its 2-10 keV X-ray flux is highly variable [arXiv:1106.3023], and it exhibits a steep radio spectral slope [arXiv:2006.01700].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1511 2119 as a radio-bright compact-core source with below-average black-hole mass, NVSS counterpart, steep spectra, and a VLA A-configuration slightly extended morphology.

quotes.quote: J1511 2119 This source has a very compact core at kpc-scale (Fig. t left panel) with a flux density of S_ int 18.19 mJy. It is a radio bright object with L_ int 39.66 above the average, but has a small black hole with (M_ BH / M_ ) 6.63 below the average. It was detected at 1.4 GHz with an associated counterpart of NVSS J151159 211900. The flux density and luminosity are S_ 1.4 46.9 mJy and L_ 1.4 39.48 respectively. The spectral slope is steep with _ in-band 0.81 and _ 1.4-5.5 0.69.

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 15091-2107 as a Seyfert/NLSy1-like source with a moderately broad Fe-line profile and an elliptical radio host.

quotes.quote: In Osterbrock De Robertis (1985), IRAS 15091-2107 is classified as a type 1 Seyfert, notwithstanding that the HI emission lines are only slightly broader that the forbidden lines and FeII emission is as strong as observed in NLSy1.
The only exceptions are IRAS 15091-2107 and IGR J16185-5928, where the 2-10 keV flux is more variable than the soft flux.

In IRAS 15091-2107 the line profile is consistent with a moderately broad Gaussian component, while between the two XMM- Newton observations of IGR J16385-2057 the line has varied its energy; also, marginal evidence for a narrow line at 7 keV (EW = 60 ^ +50 _ 40 eV) in both observations suggest the presence of variable ionized Fe lines in this source.

sources.summary: The paper identifies warm absorption as the best single-component model for IRAS 15091-2107, while noting prior work reported both cold and warm absorbers.

quotes.quote: For NGC 4593, IRAS 15091-2107, Mrk 766 and ESO 323-G077, the AIC indicates that warm absorption is the best-fitting scenario.

IRAS 15091-2107 JB2007 reports a cold and a warm absorber in this source. We find our best fitted model to be the one with a warm absorption since we do not consider a combination of both warm and cold absorption.

gmw_00003394.name: object 1
gmw_00003394.overview: Object 1 is a faint galaxy identified as a gamma-ray burst host candidate [arXiv:1303.4186]. Photometric modeling of its near-infrared detections suggests the galaxy is best fit by a template representing an old stellar population at a redshift between 0.38 and 1.96 [arXiv:1303.4186].

sources.summary: The paper identifies object 1 as the GRB host candidate, gives its coordinates, reports a point-like NIR detection, and models its photometry to constrain redshift and galaxy type.

quotes.quote: We therefore identify the \ host galaxy candidate with object 1. The latter is located at R.A. = 20^ h 15^ m 19.81^ s Dec. = +15^ 31^ 02.5^ , and its measured magnitudes based on both datasets are reported in Table tab:expo . The source appears to be point-like in all frames where it is detected, although its faintness would have probably prevented any extension be measured.

We have combined all our photometric observations from TNG and CFHT to produce a wide coverage spectral energy distribution ( ugrizYJHK ) for the putative host of , see Table tab:expo . We have corrected it for the observed galactic extinction ( E_ B-V = 0.16 ), and using these data and the photometric redshift code by fernandez99 , we have estimated the redshift and basic properties of our host galaxy candidate, object 1. As shown in Table tab:expo , object 1 is not detected in our ugriz images, and it is marginally seen in all the observed NIR bands ( YJHK , in all of them between 3 and 5 sigma significance). At face value this may point to a very high-redshift solution ( z 7 ), but such a galaxy would hardly be detectable in our data. Excluding this very high redshift solution, the best-fit candidate is represented by a galaxy with an old stellar population template with redshift z [ 0.38, 1.96 ] .

gmw_00003395.name: J0651+2734
gmw_00003395.overview: J0651+2734 is a companion galaxy located near the radio galaxy B2 0648+27 [arXiv:astro-ph/0606303]. It has a total neutral hydrogen mass of 80 million solar masses and exhibits a faint gas extension pointing toward its neighbor [arXiv:astro-ph/0606303].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0651+2734 as a nearby companion to B2 0648+27, gives its H I mass, and notes a possible gas extension toward the radio galaxy.

quotes.quote: The companion J0651+2734 ( 4) is a relatively nearby companion, both in radial velocity and spatial distance. The total \ mass of J0651+2734 is M_ = 8 10^ 8 M_ .
