Such retrograde encounters can excite odd modes, especially the n_r = 1 mode, in discs. If these waves are indeed recently produced by an interaction, then the many dust lanes and feathers visible in the Hubble Heritage image of this object could be the result of gas clouds trying to pursue eccentric orbits cutting through the waves.

gmw_00003488.name: SGC 2311.8 4353
gmw_00003488.overview: SGC 2311.8 4353 is a low-surface-brightness companion galaxy to the giant spiral NGC 7531 [arXiv:1304.3529]. Although it is a dwarf in luminosity, the galaxy is large in size and is likely undergoing tidal disruption by its host [arXiv:1304.3529].

sources.summary: The paper describes SGC 2311.8 4353 as a low-surface-brightness companion to NGC 7531, dwarf in luminosity but large, and likely tidally disrupting.

quotes.quote: Figure glsbs shows other examples: Malin 2, UGC 6614, UGC 1230, and in addition a strange possibly unrelated case called SGC 2311.8 4353, which was first studied in detail by Buta (1987). SGC 2311.8 4353 is a very low surface brightness companion to the giant spiral NGC 7531, and is notable for having an isophotal diameter 2/3 the size of NGC 7531. If at the same distance as NGC 7531, SGC 2311.8 4353 would be a dwarf in luminosity but not in size. That is, it would be a large low surface brightness galaxy, but not a giant. In a recent study of tidal streams in late-type spirals, Mart inez-Delgado et al. (2010) have interpreted SGC 2311.8 4353 as a companion in the act of tidal disruption by NGC 7531.

gmw_00003489.name: SDSS J140831 000737
gmw_00003489.overview: SDSS J140831 000737 is a low surface brightness spiral galaxy containing a bulge and a bar [arXiv:astro-ph/0310644]. Its structure features a stepped disk with distinct inner and outer exponential components, each displaying different spiral arm patterns [arXiv:astro-ph/0310644].

sources.summary: The paper highlights SDSS J140831 000737 as a stepped-disk LSB galaxy with inner and outer exponential disks, distinct spiral patterns, a bar and bulge, and faint outer arms detected in SDSS imaging.

quotes.quote: An interesting galaxy offering such an opportunity is SDSS J140831 00073 (1405 + 0006 in ISIB96). This galaxy has an unusual structure reminiscent of the multi-component disks mentioned by BF93 . The SBP of the disk of this galaxy is approximated best by two exponentials, corresponding to an ``inner'' and an ``outer'' disk. This galaxy was classified by ISIB96 as Sb(). On the SDSS images, a bulge, a bar, and different spiral patterns in the ``inner'' disk and in the ``outer'' disk are clearly seen (Fig. \ fig:outer_disk ).

At a surface brightness of (g^*) 24.5 mag arcsec ^ 2 ( R >  15 ), the ``inner'' spiral arms disappear. This surface brightness limit also corresponds to the apparent size of this galaxy given in previous studies, and to the outer limit of the ``inner'' disk. On the SDSS images we found a new subsystem of spiral arms fainter than (g^*) 25.0 mag arcsec ^ 2 .

gmw_00003490.name: object 2
gmw_00003490.overview: The galaxy Q 1323-0021 is an absorbing galaxy [arXiv:1807.01755] identified as a damped Lyman-alpha host at a redshift of 0.71645 [arXiv:1711.00407]. It is classified as a moderately early-type galaxy with a very low star formation rate [arXiv:1009.0223].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the Q 1323-0021 absorbing galaxy after SPSF subtraction, confirms real residual flux, and extracts its spectral features while avoiding quasar-PSF bias.

quotes.quote: Two additional objects are identified as the absorbing galaxies after performing SPSF subtraction (Q 1323-0021 and Q 2328+0022, see Figure fig:SPSFsub ). For these two systems, we vary the size and limits of the wavelength windows to see if the residual flux is real, or an artefact of the subtraction. In all realisations of the SPSF-subtraction, the residual flux is visible, which confirms that the signal is real (see Figure fig:SPSFsub ).

1D-spectra covering the recovered spectral features of the absorbing galaxies are then extracted with a simple extraction using standard IRAF tasks. By using a simple extraction, we avoid biasing the flux-measurement by the signal gradient present in the wing of the quasar PSF Moller2000a ; an effect which would result from the standard optimal extraction weighting scheme Horne1986 . We define the extraction apertures relative to the quasar trace, with aperture limits [ +0.38'':+1.26'' ] and [ +0.38'':+1.64'' ] for Q 1323-0021 and Q 2328+0022, respectively.

sources.summary: In the Q1323 0021 absorber field, object 2 is a previously detected nearby galaxy; the paper finds no H-alpha emission, inferring a moderately early type and very low star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: After PSF subtraction of the QSO, the nearby object is more visible (labelled 2 in Figure Fig:Fig1 , and is in the same position as the object that was detected in the K band IR imaging of this QSO in HW07 and adaptive optics imaging of Chun10 . The quality of the PSF subtraction of the QSO is not adequate to obtain a magnitude of this object in the i band image, and we note only its position in Table Tab:Mags . It is interesting to note that no H- emission is detected in this galaxy Per10 . The lack of H- emission and the non-detections in the g and images of this field (even though the seeing and depths were similar) indicates that this galaxy is of moderately early type, with few young stars producing emission in the near UV and u band at the redshift of the absorber. Indeed, Per10 determine a SFR of < 0.1M _ yr ^ 1 for this object based on the non-detection of H- .

sources.summary: The paper identifies ALMA J132323.81-002154.1 as a CO-emitting DLA host at z=0.71645 and compares its DLA-selected properties with flux-selected galaxy scaling relations.

quotes.quote: Above we have reported the discovery of a CO line emitting galaxy (ALMA J132323.81-002154.1) at z_ CO = 0.71645 and at the position of a known optical candidate DLA host. In this section we now compare the properties of this DLA galaxy to the properties of emission-selected galaxy samples. In particular, we ask if it lies within the range of the scaling relations known from flux-selected samples.

gmw_00003491.name: object 5
gmw_00003491.overview: Object 5 is one of the brightest galaxies in its region and is part of a dense galaxy association [arXiv:1009.0223, arXiv:1807.01755]. It exhibits a flat spectral energy distribution characteristic of active star formation [arXiv:1009.0223].

sources.summary: In the Q1436 0051 field, object 5 is one of the brightest nearby galaxies, with a flat star-forming spectral energy distribution and a photometric redshift consistent with the absorber group.

quotes.quote: Objects labelled 5, 6 and 7 are the brightest galaxies in the region, with m_i =23.86, m_i=20.90 and m_i=21.89 . Object 6 and 7 appear to be interacting. All of these three objects have relatively flat spectral energy distributions, typical of actively star forming galaxies at the redshift of the absorbers. Photometric redshifts for these three objects determine z_ phot 0.70 , consistent with these being members of a group. We note that the 1 error on the photometric redshift of object 5 is 0.11 , i.e. the photometric redshift determined from hyperz is only 2 away from the redshift of the higher z system at =0.9281.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Object 5 as the likely higher-redshift Q 1436-0051 absorber counterpart among multiple galaxies sharing the slit in a dense association.

quotes.quote: Galaxy associations in the Q 1436-0051 field Past observations indicate that absorbing galaxies can be related to galaxy associations in dense environments Kacprzak2010, Rao2011, Christensen2014, Peroux2017 . We find a remarkable example of such associations in the field Q 1436-0051, which is observed to host multiple galaxies that coincide with the redshifts of two damped absorbers ( _ 10 [ N _ H i ( cm ^ 2 ) ] = 20.08^ +0.10 _ 0.12 and 18.40 0.98 , consistent with the lower limit of the definition for a sub-DLA, at redshifts z_ abs =0.737 and 0.928 , respectively). We confirm the presence of three galaxies at the low- z , and two galaxies at the high-z, all remarkably lying within a single slit-position. We show the spatial configuration of these systems in the 2D FORS2 spectrum in the Appendix appendix_Q1436-0051 , Figure fig:Q1436-0051_groupassociations . Based on impact-parameter and redshift information [ b , z_ em ] of the objects in the slit (see Table tab:appendix_Q1436-0051 ), we find the most likely counterparts to be located at [ 45.5, 0.73749 ] and [ 34.9, 0.92886 ] , which is consistent with Object 6 and Object 5, respectively, assuming the nomenclature of and . We also note that the host of the z_ abs = 0.737 absorber is likely part of an interacting system.

gmw_00003492.name: Object 6
gmw_00003492.overview: Object 6 is a bright, actively star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.737 [arXiv:1807.01755, arXiv:1009.0223]. It is part of a dense galaxy association and appears to be interacting with a neighboring galaxy [arXiv:1807.01755, arXiv:1009.0223].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Object 6 as the likely lower-redshift Q 1436-0051 absorber counterpart, one of several galaxies in a dense association and probably interacting.

quotes.quote: Galaxy associations in the Q 1436-0051 field Past observations indicate that absorbing galaxies can be related to galaxy associations in dense environments Kacprzak2010, Rao2011, Christensen2014, Peroux2017 . We find a remarkable example of such associations in the field Q 1436-0051, which is observed to host multiple galaxies that coincide with the redshifts of two damped absorbers ( _ 10 [ N _ H i ( cm ^ 2 ) ] = 20.08^ +0.10 _ 0.12 and 18.40 0.98 , consistent with the lower limit of the definition for a sub-DLA, at redshifts z_ abs =0.737 and 0.928 , respectively). We confirm the presence of three galaxies at the low- z , and two galaxies at the high-z, all remarkably lying within a single slit-position. We show the spatial configuration of these systems in the 2D FORS2 spectrum in the Appendix appendix_Q1436-0051 , Figure fig:Q1436-0051_groupassociations . Based on impact-parameter and redshift information [ b , z_ em ] of the objects in the slit (see Table tab:appendix_Q1436-0051 ), we find the most likely counterparts to be located at [ 45.5, 0.73749 ] and [ 34.9, 0.92886 ] , which is consistent with Object 6 and Object 5, respectively, assuming the nomenclature of and . We also note that the host of the z_ abs = 0.737 absorber is likely part of an interacting system.

sources.summary: Object 6 in the Q1436 0051 field is a bright star-forming galaxy at the absorber-group photometric redshift and appears to be interacting with object 7.

quotes.quote: Objects labelled 5, 6 and 7 are the brightest galaxies in the region, with m_i =23.86, m_i=20.90 and m_i=21.89 . Object 6 and 7 appear to be interacting. All of these three objects have relatively flat spectral energy distributions, typical of actively star forming galaxies at the redshift of the absorbers. Photometric redshifts for these three objects determine z_ phot 0.70 , consistent with these being members of a group.

gmw_00003493.name: Object 7
gmw_00003493.overview: Object 7 is a bright, actively star-forming galaxy located in the Q1436 0051 field [arXiv:1009.0223]. It is a member of a galaxy group and appears to be interacting with a neighboring galaxy, Object 6 [arXiv:1009.0223].

sources.summary: Object 7 in the Q1436 0051 field is a bright star-forming galaxy at the absorber-group photometric redshift and appears to be interacting with object 6.

quotes.quote: Objects labelled 5, 6 and 7 are the brightest galaxies in the region, with m_i =23.86, m_i=20.90 and m_i=21.89 . Object 6 and 7 appear to be interacting. All of these three objects have relatively flat spectral energy distributions, typical of actively star forming galaxies at the redshift of the absorbers. Photometric redshifts for these three objects determine z_ phot 0.70 , consistent with these being members of a group.

gmw_00003494.name: J091954+291345
gmw_00003494.overview: The galaxy J091954+291345 is a spiral galaxy that clearly exhibits spiral arms in its Ks-band image [arXiv:1901.11182]. Based on its rotation curve and spiral structure, the galaxy's disk has a negative inclination, which constrains its radial inflow speed to at most 30 km/s [arXiv:1901.11182].

sources.summary: The paper resolves spiral structure in J091954+291345, infers a negative disk inclination, and uses that tilt to limit radial inflow to at most 30 km/s.

quotes.quote: The galaxy J091954+291345 clearly shows the spiral arms in the K_s image. Together with the direction of disk rotation determined from the rotation curve, we deduce that the disk has a negative inclination. A disk without a vertical velocity gradient can explain the over 300 \ LOS velocity range, but that would require a 500-kpc thick ``disk'' ( \ = 250 kpc) to match the blue absorption end. With = 10 kpc, a significantly thinner disk of \ = 15 kpc (left panel of Figure  fig:j0919_model ) can explain the same velocity range. Further reducing \ would make the absorption not blue enough to match the measurement.

On the other hand, a negatively inclined disk can reproduce the measured velocity range with radial inflow of no more than 30 \ (right panel); otherwise the absorption would not be red enough at the disk midplane. Because the two disk tilts permit significantly different radial inflow scenarios, the deduced negative disk inclination tightens the constraint on the radial inflow speed to at most 30 .

gmw_00003495.name: J102907+421737
gmw_00003495.overview: J102907+421737 is a spiral galaxy featuring a blue outer disk that suggests inside-out disk growth [arXiv:1901.11182]. Based on the wrapping direction of its spiral arms and disk rotation, the galaxy has an inferred negative inclination that limits its radial inflow speed to at most 40 km/s [arXiv:1901.11182].

sources.summary: The paper finds spiral arms and a blue outer disk in J102907+421737, infers negative inclination, and limits radial inflow to at most 40 km/s.

quotes.quote: For J102907+421737, the F390W image shows the spiral arms most clearly, and we visually enhance the spiral features and create the color image as described in Section  ssec:disk_tilt . The color image also shows that the outer disk is bluer than the inner disk. This suggests that the outer disk is younger than the inner disk deJong1996,Bell2000,MacArthur2004 , indicating an inside-out disk growth. From the revealed wrapping direction of the spiral arms and the measured disk rotation, we deduce that the disk has a negative inclination.

If we also consider radial inflow on the \ = 10 kpc disk, then the inflow speed for a positively (negatively) inclined disk cannot exceed 80 (40) . Otherwise, the absorption becomes not blue enough (too blue) at the disk midplane; see the middle (right) panel. Hence, the inferred negative disk inclination limits the radial inflow speed to at most 40 .

gmw_00003496.name: J123049+071050
gmw_00003496.overview: J123049+071050 is a spiral galaxy with a positively inclined rotating disk [arXiv:1901.11182]. High-resolution imaging resolves the system into this main galaxy, which displays weak traces of spiral arms, and a possible satellite companion [arXiv:1901.11182].

sources.summary: The paper resolves J123049+071050 into a spiral galaxy plus possible satellite, infers positive disk inclination, but finds disk tilt cannot constrain radial inflow.

quotes.quote: The high-resolution K_s image resolves the target ``galaxy'' into two objects: a galaxy with spiral arms and a possible satellite. The galaxy also has weak traces of spiral arms, and we visually enhance the arms as described in Section  ssec:disk_tilt . From the wrapping direction of the spiral arms and the direction of disk rotation, we deduce that the disk has a positive inclination. A rotating disk without a vertical velocity gradient can reproduce the 160 \ measured velocity range, but the ``disk'' has to be 260-kpc thick ( \ = 130 kpc) to bring the red absorption end close to the galaxy systemic velocity. In contrast, the fiducial model with a vertical velocity gradient only requires a 10-kpc thick disk ( \ = 5 kpc) to reproduce the velocity range spanned by the absorption.

Knowing the disk tilt a priori cannot rule out radial inflow nor constrain the radial inflow speed for this galaxy. Even as the inflow speed increases from zero to 300 , the LOS velocities at both the bluest and the reddest ends change by no more than 20 . This 20 \ shift is allowed by the measurement uncertainties.

gmw_00003497.name: J124601+173152
gmw_00003497.overview: J124601+173152 is an irregular, clumpy galaxy that lacks a typical axisymmetric disk morphology [arXiv:1901.11182]. It features two optical clumps but only a single near-infrared nucleus [arXiv:1901.11182], and it resides in a rich environment with three brighter photometric-redshift neighbors [arXiv:1901.09123].

sources.summary: The paper describes J124601+173152 as an irregular, clumpy system with two optical clumps and a single near-infrared nucleus rather than a normal disk.

quotes.quote: Both F390W and F814W images of J124601+173152 reveal that the galaxy has an irregular, clumpy structure. In both F390W and F814W images, the galaxy shows two bright clumps along the N-S direction. However, the K_s image only detects a single nucleus that lies at the location of the southern clump. The color image also shows that the emission in K_s overlaps with the southern clump only. Altogether, our images suggest that this galaxy has a complicated, clumpy structure instead of a typical, axisymmetric disk morphology.

sources.summary: The paper places 124601+173152 in the richest local environment, with three brighter photometric-redshift neighbors, possible infall into an overdensity, and ambiguous absorption-host identification.

quotes.quote: The richest environment is likely that of 124601+173152. The environment of target 124601+173152 includes three brighter galaxies with consistent photometric redshifts, but our target is the closet the quasar sightline. Our target may be falling into the galaxy overdensity marked by 124559+173203 (40 4 W, V_ LOS = 1010 ). flagged 124601+173152 because the host identification for the \ absorption in the 124601+173156 sightline was not unique.

gmw_00003498.name: J142459+382113
gmw_00003498.overview: The spiral galaxy J142459+382113 features a negatively inclined disk with clearly detected spiral arms [arXiv:1901.11182]. It exhibits an inside-out growth pattern, indicated by an outer disk that is bluer, younger, and less metal-rich than its inner disk [arXiv:1901.11182].

sources.summary: The paper detects spiral arms and inside-out color gradients in J142459+382113, infers negative inclination, and rules out radial inflow along the sightline.

quotes.quote: Both F390W and F814W images of J142459+382113 unambiguously detect the spiral arms. From the wrapping direction of the arms and the measured disk rotation, we deduce the disk as being negatively inclined. Moreover, the color image clearly shows that the outer disk is bluer than the inner disk. The presence of the color gradient is also supported by the larger disk scalelengths measured at the bluer bands. The color gradient indicates that the outer disk has a younger stellar population and a lower metallicity than the inner disk, suggesting the disk grows inside-out.

Radial inflow makes the absorption even bluer than disk rotation alone, because both velocity components have the same Doppler sign near the midplane. The right panel illustrates this problem using a fiducial inflow speed of 100 . Therefore, for this galaxy, its negatively inclined disk excludes the detection of radial inflow in the sightline.

gmw_00003499.name: NGC 5170
gmw_00003499.overview: NGC 5170 is a quiescent, edge-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:0911.4743, arXiv:astro-ph/0512485] that hosts an X-shaped bulge [arXiv:2210.12255] and a rich globular cluster system [arXiv:0911.4743]. The galaxy exhibits mild mid-plane asymmetry [arXiv:2210.12255] and shows no signs of starburst activity or an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0512485].

sources.summary: HST/ACS imaging of edge-on spiral NGC 5170 finds a rich globular-cluster system, a blue tilt in its metal-poor clusters, and a possible associated ultra-compact dwarf.

quotes.quote: Here we present HST/ACS imaging, in the B and I bands, of the edge-on Sb/Sc galaxy NGC 5170. Excluding the central disk region region, we detect a 142 objects with colours and sizes typical of globular clusters (GCs). Our main result is the discovery of a `blue tilt' (a mass-metallicity relation), at the 3 level, in the metal-poor GC subpopulation of this Milky Way like galaxy. The tilt is consistent with that seen in massive elliptical galaxies and with the self enrichment model of Bailin Harris. For a linear mass-metallicity relation, the tilt has the form Z L ^ 0.42 0.13 .

We derive a total GC system population of 600 100, making it much richer than the Milky Way. However when this number is normalised by the host galaxy luminosity or stellar mass it is similar to that of M31. Finally, we report the presence of a potential Ultra Compact Dwarf of size 6 pc and luminosity M _I -12.5, assuming it is physically associated with NGC 5170.

sources.summary: The paper reports a bright, round object near NGC 5170 with red color and UCD-like size and luminosity, but notes that its classification and association require confirmation.

quotes.quote: Our ACS imaging field includes a bright object which is excluded from our final GC list. Its magnitude I _o = 19.00 and its size of 0.065 ^ '' (in both B and I filters) corresponds to are M _I = --12.45 and _h = 6.14 pc for a distance of 19.5 Mpc. It has a relatively red colour (B--I) _o = 1.89 and it appears quite round in the ACS images. It is located at R.A. = 13:29:53.4 and Dec. = --17:59:09.1 (J2000), SE of the galaxy disk (see Figures 1 and 5).

Whether this object should be classified as a massive GC or an Ultra Compact Dwarf is a matter of debate. The properties of objects classified as UCDs overlap with those that have been classified as massive GCs (e.g. Forbes et al. 2008). Indeed the properties of the object described above lie within the range of those found for GCs in the rich GC systems studied by Harris (2009a). So although the object is clearly distinct from the bulk of the NGC 5170 GCs in terms of its luminosity, it is still consistent with being drawn from a (universal) GC luminosity function.

sources.summary: The paper identifies mild mid-plane asymmetry in NGC 5170, describing its ring structures, prominent X-shaped B/P bulge, asymmetric red contours, and a clear asymmetry-profile peak.

quotes.quote: NGC 5170 is a S0/a galaxy, hosting an inner ring-lens and a near-outer ring made of spiral arms. A prominent X shape, associated with its B/P bulge, is clearly visible from the unsharp mask in Fig.  fig:sample , together with evidence of ansae just beyond it Buta2015 . The B/P bulge has a small extension compared to the host galaxy. The mid-plane asymmetry diagnostics are shown in Fig.  fig:sample and Fig.  fig:AMP_real_galaxies , top-right panel.

The red contours appear asymmetric with respect to the mid-plane, extending to x 3.3 kpc. Further out, a mild mid-plane asymmetry in the surface brightness is visible, extending to |x| 6.5 kpc. This asymmetry disappears in the disc region on the left side at x<-6.5 kpc, while it remains weak and extends further out on the left side at x>6.5 kpc. The corresponding mid-plane asymmetry profile is calculated adopting z_ min =0.5 kpc and z_ max =2.5 kpc and it shows a clear peak reaching A _ (x) 0.04 , at x 4 kpc, which however does not present a complementary one at x 4 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5170 as a less massive nearby Sc comparison galaxy, describing it as quiescent and edge-on, with no diffuse halo emission found using the same Chandra analysis.

quotes.quote: We also studied a similar, but less massive galaxy, NGC 5170, as a test of our procedure. The galaxies are massive and nearby (NGC 5746 is an SBb galaxy at d =29.4 Mpc and has V_c =307 5 km/s; NGC 5170 is an Sc galaxy at d =24.0 Mpc and has V_c =250 5 km/s). Both galaxies are quiescent, showing no signs of either starburst activity, interaction with other galaxies, or an active galactic nucleus. The disks of the galaxies are viewed almost perfectly edge-on.
The same observing technique and data analysis revealed no diffuse emission around the less massive galaxy NGC 5170.

gmw_00003500.name: Minkowski's Object
gmw_00003500.overview: Minkowski's Object is a peculiar starburst dwarf galaxy interacting with a radio jet from the nearby elliptical galaxy NGC 541 [arXiv:1703.03006, arXiv:astro-ph/0510261]. This interaction has triggered highly efficient star formation [arXiv:1703.03006, arXiv:1106.3434], supported by the first detections of molecular gas in the system [arXiv:1703.03006].

sources.summary: The paper uses ALMA to detect molecular gas in Minkowski's Object, a dwarf galaxy struck by a radio jet, and finds efficient upstream star formation consistent with positive AGN feedback.

quotes.quote: We use ALMA to detect and image CO (1-0) emission from Minkowski's Object, a dwarf galaxy that is interacting with a radio jet from a nearby elliptical galaxy. These observations are the first to detect molecular gas in Minkowski's Object. We estimate the range in the mass of molecular gas in Minkowski's Object assuming two different values of the ratio of the molecular gas mass to the CO luminosity, _ CO . For the Milky Way value of _ CO =4.6 M_ (K km s^ 1 pc^2)^ 1 we obtain a molecular gas mass of M_ H_2 =3.0 10^7 M_ , 6 of the H I gas mass. We also use the prescription of Narayanan et al. (2012) to estimate an _ CO =27 M_ (K km s^ 1 pc^2)^ 1 , in which case we obtain M_ H_2 =1.8 10^8 M_ , 36 of the H I mass.

The observations are consistent with previous claims of star formation being induced in Minkowski's Object via the passage of the radio jet, and it therefore being a rare local example of positive feedback from an AGN. In particular, we find highly efficient star formation, with gas depletion timescales 5 10^7 - 3 10^8 yr (for assumed values of _ CO =4.6 and 27 M_ (K km s^ 1 pc^2)^ 1 , respectively) in the upstream regions of Minkowski's Object that were struck first by the jet, and less efficient star formation downstream. We discuss the implications of this observation for models of jet induced star formation and radio mode feedback in massive galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper presents Minkowski's Object as a peculiar starburst galaxy at the end of NGC 541's jet, with H I and morphology suggesting jet-triggered star formation or radiative cooling.

quotes.quote: Minkowski's Object is a peculiar starburst galaxy (at z = 0.0187) at end of the jet from NGC 541 (van Breugel et al 1985; van Breugel et al. 2004; Croft et al. 2004). This object is an ideal candidate for a detailed study of the effects of the interaction between the radio plasma and the ISM. Its morphology is strongly suggestive of a collision of a low luminosity FR-I type jet from NGC 541 with a gas rich cloud/object (see Fig. 7 and van Breugel et al. 1985).

Recent C-array VLA observations have shown that a cloud of \ is detected just down-stream from the main star formation region (see Fig. 7). This cloud has a total \ mass of 3 10^8 M _ (corresponding to a M _ / L _B 0.17 ), it has the same transverse size as the radio jet and consists of two main components separated at the centre of the radio jet. A velocity gradient in the \ of 60 \ is observed. Although, as in the case of Centaurus A, \ is detected close to the location of the star formation and radio jet, it is not clear whether the same scenario can explain the case of the Minkowski's Object. In this object the radio jet itself may be the cause of the formation of the \ as predicted in the jet-triggered radiative cooling model (Fragile et al. 2004).

sources.summary: Minkowski Object is described as a peculiar star-forming object near NGC 541, undetected in CO but showing very short depletion time and high star-formation efficiency.

quotes.quote: Minkowski Object (MO) is a star-forming peculiar object near the double-lobed FR- 1 radio source NGC 541, in the galaxy cluster Abell 194 Croft_2006 . In projection, MO is located in a large optical bridge which connects NGC 541 with the interacting galaxies NGC 545/547, suggesting that gas of MO may have origins in previous interactions between these galaxies. MO has recently been observed but not detected in CO(1-0) with the Plateau de Bureau interferometer (Nesvadba et al. in prep, private communication).

MO and 09.6 have not been detected in CO by the 30m-telescope. However, we reached interesting rms for these two sources, leading to upper limits of molecular gas amount of 10^7-10^8 M_ . This means that 09.6 and MO have a depletion time of 1 Gyr and 0.02 Gyr respectively. In a KS-diagram, 09.6 lies with or above the normal star forming galaxies and MO with the highly efficient star forming objects.

sources.summary: The paper studies Minkowski’s Object as a star-forming dwarf-like system near NGC541, finding its X-ray emission likely dominated by HMXBs and its starburst plausibly triggered by interaction with NGC541.

quotes.quote: Minkowski's Object (MO) minkowski58 lies along the jet emanating from NGC541 (3C 40A), and is believed to be a classic example of jet-triggered star-formation in the local Universe. MO was studied by breugel85 and brodie85 , who suggested that the observed emission originated in a starburst. More recently a multi-wavelength study by croft06 found that the stellar population of MO is dominated by a 7.5 10^6 years old, 1.9 10^7 \ M_ instantaneous burst, with a current SFR of 0.52 \ M_ /yr .

Thus, the estimated age of MO is (0.75-3.7) 10^7 years, which is in approximate agreement with the time range of the possible interaction between MO and NGC541. Thus, it is feasible that the interaction between a preexisting gas-rich dwarf galaxy (now observed as MO) and NGC541 triggered a starburst event, which largely rejuvenated the stellar population of MO.

sources.summary: The paper models Minkowski's Object as a peculiar star-forming system bombarded by NGC 541's FR I jet, arguing the jet likely drives its cold gas and star formation.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we focus on the case of M.O., a peculiar star forming object located at a redshift of z = 0.0189 (C06) that is currently being bombarded by a FR I radio jet from the nearby galaxy NGC 541. The M.O. system is of particular interest due to the lack of evidence for an especially dense ISM or IGM. There is also not much evidence for cold gas outside of the jet interaction site, unlike in Centaurus A. As a result, it is unlikely that significant star formation would proceed in M.O.

sources.summary: The paper presents multiwavelength observations of Minkowski's Object, describing it as a star-forming peculiar galaxy whose young burst, UV morphology, H I cloud, and H II regions support jet-triggered star formation.

quotes.quote: We present neutral hydrogen, ultraviolet, optical and near-infrared imaging, and optical spectroscopy, of Minkowski's Object (MO), a star forming peculiar galaxy near NGC 541. The observations strengthen evidence that star formation in MO was triggered by the radio jet from NGC 541. Key new results are the discovery of a 4.9 10^8 \ double \ cloud straddling the radio jet downstream from MO, where the jet changes direction and decollimates; strong detections of MO, also showing double structure, in UV and ; and numerous \ regions and associated clusters in MO.

In UV, MO resembles the radio-aligned, rest-frame UV morphologies in many high redshift radio galaxies (HzRGs), also thought to be caused by jet-induced star formation. MO's stellar population is dominated by a 7.5 Myr-old, 1.9 10^7 \ instantaneous burst, with current star formation rate 0.52 (concentrated upstream from where the \ column density is high).

gmw_00003501.name: MCG-02-02-086
gmw_00003501.overview: MCG-02-02-086 is the brightest cluster galaxy of Abell 85 [arXiv:1506.08560, arXiv:2207.11287]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus with bipolar radio jets [arXiv:2012.10774], a LINER optical signature [arXiv:1405.7758], and a small young stellar population indicating recent central star formation [arXiv:1401.1822].

sources.summary: The paper presents VLA, Chandra, and Gemini evidence that the Abell 85 BCG has bipolar AGN jets and a single core, not a resolved binary black hole.

quotes.quote: With a resolution of 0. 02, this radio image shows two kiloparsec-scale bipolar jets emanating from the active galactic nucleus of the Abell 85 BCG. The galaxy core appears as a single entity on the new radio map. It has been assumed that the Abell 85 BCG contained a binary black hole in its core. However, Chandra X-ray data and the new high-resolution radio map show no evidence that the Abell 85 BCG harbors a binary black hole.
The VLA image of the Abell 85 BCG shows a single core and no evidence of double nuclei. Similarly, the X-ray image only shows diffuse X-ray emission in the core of the galaxy.

The null detection of binary black holes in the X-ray and very high resolution radio data implies that the central massive object in the Abell 85 BCG is likely a single entity. Should a binary AGN exist in the Abell 85 BCG, its detection would require very long baseline interferometry (VLBI) given that the pair would be separated by less than 400 pc.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes Holm 15A and argues that its light profile is best fit without a depleted core, instead using a low-index profile plus an exponential halo.

quotes.quote: Here we investigate the unusually large ( R_ = 0.5 = 4.57 kpc) depleted core recently reported for Holm 15A, the brightest cluster galaxy of Abell 85. We model the 1D light profile, and also the 2D image (using , a tool for fitting the model in 2D). We find good agreement between the 1D and 2D analyses, with minor discrepancies attributable to intrinsic ellipticity gradients. We show that a simple profile (with a low index n and no depleted core) plus the known outer exponential ``halo'' provide a good description of the stellar distribution.

We find that the model does not provide an appropriate representation of the galaxy light distribution. In particular, in the 1D description the core has a slight excess of light, while in the 2D model the inner power-law of the fit does not represent an actual real core, but rather compensates for the ellipticity gradient ( Fit comparison and choice of best-fit model ). We therefore conclude that the galaxy is core- less and we favor the idea that its light distribution is best described by a simple profile with a low index n plus an exponential ``halo'' component as included by LC14.

sources.summary: The paper models Holm15A, a massive BCG in Abell 85, subtracts its light to find background structures, and models its gravitational lensing and central mass profile.

quotes.quote: Holm15A is the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) of Abell 85 Abell+1989 with a redshift of z=0.055 Lopez-Cruz+2014 . Its surface brightness profile has one of the largest cores ever observed that supports the idea that it contains one of the largest SMBH Mehrgan+2019 estimate an SMBH mass of 4 10^ 10 M_ . ever found Lopez-Cruz+2014,Mehrgan+2019 . However, the nature of the SMBH of Holm15A is not completely understood.

After this correction, we model the surface brightness (SB) distribution of Holm15A across the FoV of the MUSE WFM. For this purpose, we first run pyPipe3 over the full datacube to obtain the cube of the stellar model of Holm15A.

On the other hand, the drop in the measured eccentricity of the isophotes in the inner radii of Holm15A (Figure fig:fit_prof ) can be related to the dynamical mixing of the SMBH Lopez-Cruz+2014 . Therefore, the value of _e could be used as a tracer of the SOI: its total mass contained within this scale is 8.1 10^ 10 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper identifies MCG-02-02-086 as an Abell 0085 central galaxy without a cD envelope, with nuclear H emission and a small young component supporting recent central star formation.

quotes.quote: MCG-02-02-086 -- Abell 0085 This CCG is hosted by a cooling flow cluster (see Table table:objects ), but lacks a cD envelope (as summarised in Fisher, Illingworth Franx 1995). It showed nuclear H emission in McDonald et al. \ (2010), and a large ring can be seen in the X-ray image (but without an H counterpart), resembling the outer edge of a bubble (McDonald et al. \ 2010). Based on the high H /NUV ratio, McDonald et al. \ (2010) postulated that the gas could be ionised by a recent burst of star formation in the CCG centre. Similarly to ESO541-013, the ^ 2 values of the SSP and CSP fits do not differ significantly, but the multiple component fitting was found to be more consistent. It showed that the majority of the bins consisted of two components, a large, very old component (bordering the upper limit of the stellar populations model at 20 Gyr), and a very small young component (of less than 1 Gyr contributing 5 to 10 per cent of the light). This agrees with the scenario McDonald et al. \ (2010) postulated above.

sources.summary: The paper models Holm 15A as Abell 85’s brightest cluster galaxy, finding an ultra-diffuse core, a 4.0e10 solar-mass black hole, and merger-remnant evidence.

quotes.quote: Holm 15A, the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) of the galaxy cluster Abell 85, has an ultra-diffuse central region, 2 mag fainter than the faintest depleted core of any early-type galaxy (ETG) that has been dynamically modelled in detail. We use orbit-based, axisymmetric Schwarzschild models to analyse the stellar kinematics of Holm 15A from new high-resolution, wide-field spectral observations obtained with MUSE at the VLT. We find a supermassive black hole (SMBH) with a mass of (4.0 0.80) 10^ 10 \ M_ at the center of Holm 15A.

We compare Holm 15A with N-body simulations of mergers between galaxies with black holes and find that the observed amount of tangential anisotropy and the shape of the light profile are consistent with a formation scenario where Holm 15A is the remnant of a merger between two ETGs with pre-existing depleted cores. We find that black hole masses in cored galaxies, including Holm 15A, scale inversely with the central stellar surface brightness and mass density, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Holm 15A as Abell 85's brightest cluster galaxy, with the largest known core, a LINER optical signature, a luminous amorphous radio source, and an inferred massive SMBH.

quotes.quote: We have found that the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) in Abell 85, A85-BCG Holm 15A , displays the largest core so far known. Its cusp radius, r_ = 4.57 0.06 kpc ( 4 26 0 06 ), is more than 18 times larger than the mean for BCGs, and 1  kpc larger than A2261-BCG , hitherto the largest-cored BCG PL12 . Holm 15A hosts the luminous amorphous radio source 0039-095B and has the optical signature of a LINER. Scaling laws indicate that this core could host a supermassive black hole (SMBH) of mass M _ (10^ 9 10^ 11 ) M _ .

sources.summary: The paper treats the Abell 85 BCG as an extreme core-type galaxy whose measured break radius and scaling relations imply a possible central black hole near 10^11 solar masses.

quotes.quote: Finally, report on the BCG in the Abell 85 cluster. So far it is the most extreme representative of a core-type galaxy with a measured break radius (cusp radius) of R_ b = 20 kpc ( R_ = 4.6 kpc ) 2014ApJ...795L..31L Note that take a different position by showing that its stellar profile can be well fitted with a classical S e rsic model without the need for a depleted core. Based on the extrapolation of the R_ break , R_ cusp and L_ def relations, its central black hole might also have a mass around 10^ 11 2014ApJ...795L..31L . It would be highly informative and important for constraining the limitations of gas accretion- and SMBH merging processes, especially with respect to their cluster environments, if the nuclear black hole masses in these four objects could be measured and compared with empirical scaling relations.

sources.summary: The paper uses Holm 15A as a cautionary precedent: an alleged 4.6 kpc depleted core was reanalyzed as coreless, weakening claims of an extreme core and ultramassive black hole.

quotes.quote: In we demonstrated that the galaxy Holm 15A, alleged to have the largest depleted core on record 4.6 kpc lopez , actually has no central deficit relative to its outer profile which also describes the inner profile. This is important because the original investigation led to (a) the claim of a massive depleted core and in turn (b) the possible presence of a truly massive 10^ 11 M_ black hole. Remarkably, our results were later independently confirmed by the study of , who also described Holm 15A as ``core-less''.
Although larger cores should be the easiest to measure, our recent study of Holm 15A revealed that misinterpretations are still possible.

sources.summary: The paper measures the A85 BCG surface-brightness profile into the intracluster light, finding profile flattening beyond 75 kpc and evidence that massive-satellite stripping built the diffuse component.

quotes.quote: We measured the radial surface brightness profiles of the brightest cluster galaxy out to the intracluster light (radius 215 kpc), for the g and i bands. We found that both the surface brightness and the color profiles become shallower beyond 75 kpc suggesting that a distinct component, the intracluster light, starts to dominate at that radius. The color of the profile at 100 kpc suggests that the buildup of the intracluster light of Abell 85 occurs by the stripping of massive ( 10^ 10 M_ ) satellites. The measured fraction of this light ranges from 8 to 30 in , depending on the definition of intracluster light chosen.

sources.summary: The paper reports JO200 as a massive GASP stripped galaxy with a significant molecular-gas tail detection, a MUSE counterpart following spiral arms, and narrow CO linewidth.

quotes.quote: For the APEX pointings centered on the supposed gas tails identified either following the extension or the tail, we obtained a significant detection in 3/8 pointings, namely A3376 S64, A3558 S167 (JO147) and A85 S64 (JO200), shown in Fig. fig:spectra_2 , together with the marginal emission in the tail of A3558 S60 (JO157), which has S/N=2.7. All the other tail pointings have a low S/N (from 1.6 to 2.4) and are characterized by a relatively narrow linewidth. In order to be consistent with our previous works GASPX,Moretti_2020 , we used the measured line fluxes to calculate then the mass using the following equation from WatsonKoda2016 : M_ H_2 M_ = 3.8 10^3 _ 10 4.3 r_ 21 0.7 ^ 1 S_ 21 dv D_L ^2 where _ 10 is the CO-to- H_2 conversion factor expressed in M_ pc^ 2 (K km s ^ 1 ) ^ 1 Bolatto2013 , r_ 21 is the CO(2-1)-to-CO(1-0) flux ratio, S_ 21 is the CO integrated line flux in Jy and D _L is the luminosity distance in Mpc. As done in GASPX we used _ 10 =4.3 , i.e. the standard Milky Way value corresponding to a conversion factor of 2 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 (K km s ^ 1 ) ^ 1 including the helium correction. As for the r_ 21 , we adopted the same value (0.79) that we used in our APEX GASPX and ALMA GASPXXII,Moretti_2020 data analysis for the sake of comparison. To take into account the systematic uncertainty on the factor, we calculated a lower limit on the mass obtained by assuming that its value is 50 lower than the canonical Milky Way factor, while higher values are less probable in massive, metal-rich galaxies Bolatto2013 . Errors calculated in this way are shown in the last column of Tab. tab:results . The molecular gas content of the sample galaxies varies between 10 ^9 and 2 10 ^ 10 in galaxies where the line is measured with sufficient S/N. The molecular gas masses calculated in this way are systematically 5 larger than those calculated using the equation, which explicitly takes into account the dependence on redshift, being M_ H_2 M_ = L'_ CO = 3.25 10^7 S_ CO v _ obs ^ 2 D_L ^2 1+z ^ 3 where S_ CO v is the velocity integrated flux in Jy , _ obs is the observed line frequency at the source redshift z, and D_L is the corresponding luminosity distance. In this formulation the major source of uncertainty comes from the conversion value, which includes both the CO-to- H_2 conversion factor and the r_ 21 line ratio, and is typically assumed to be equal to 5.5 Jachym2014 . These values are, in any case, within the errors of our measurements. This allows a straightforward comparison with the recent determination of the molecular gas content/fraction in Virgo galaxies by , who also uses the IMF to determine galaxy stellar masses. Fig. fig:fh2 shows the position of our targets in the molecular gas fraction versus stellar mass plane, where we indicated with different colors galaxies with (green) and without (magenta) star formation in the tail. The red squares refer to the 4 GASP galaxies undergoing strong RPS analyzed in . It can be immediately seen that 50 of the observed sample of selected galaxies (9/19) with asymmetries lie above the relation found for normal star forming galaxies in the field (blue dashed region from Saintonge2017 ) and in Virgo (grey dots). Among the galaxies closer to the relation for field galaxies, A85 S64 (JO200) possesses a MUSE counterpart, which follows its spiral arms. We covered this emission with a second APEX pointing, which reveals the presence of molecular gas. The morphology of this galaxy closely resembles the one of the JO201 GASP galaxy GASPII , which shows unwinding spiral arms possibly caused by the RPS GASPXXIX . If we include the molecular gas mass in the tail in the global budget, then the point would move even closer to the upper edge of the distribution of field galaxies in the M _ f _ H_2 plane (see Fig. fig:fh2 ). Among the galaxies located below the relation in Fig. fig:fh2 , only one has a clear detection (A3376 S45) indicating that this galaxy is deficient. This galaxy also shows no emission from the tail region. The other galaxies located below the Saintonge2017 relation are characterized by a low signal-to-noise (shaded triangles), irrespective of their stellar mass. One of these, the GASP galaxy JO159, has been classified as an initial stripping phase (Poggianti+, in prep.). It is clear that when the asymmetry is coupled with having SF in the tail, then in 75 (6/8) of the cases with S/N > 3 the content is enhanced within the stellar body (83 = 10/12 if we include the Moretti_2020 galaxies). In particular, galaxies classified by GASP as jellyfishes (red squares and green dots surrounded by a black square in Fig. fig:fh2 ), i.e. those having an ionized gas tail at least as long as the stellar disk and therefore being at their peak stripping phase, show a strongly enhanced molecular gas fraction. Among these, A3558 S167(GASP JO147) is the richest massive galaxy (see also Merluzzi2013 ) and, together with the galaxy JO49, also rich, it hosts an active nucleus, as the other massive jellyfishes in GASP poggianti2017,Peluso+2021 . Noticeably, also the GASP galaxy JW108, which has been classified as a truncated disk, i. e. a galaxy where the ionized gas emission comes from a region smaller than the extent of the stellar disk, typical of the latest stage of gas stripping, lies above the relation. In fact, because of the more advanced stage of stripping of this galaxy, most of the gas is probably removed or got converted into , resulting in a very faint emission (below the detection threshold) in this galaxy, while still showing an increased amount of cold molecular gas in its disk (while the detection in the tail has a low S/N). In fact, besides JO159, the only GASP galaxy not lying above the field relation is A3376 S75 (A3376 B 0261) which is a control sample galaxy in GASP and thus shows a pretty normal molecular gas content. On the other hand, when the asymmetry manifests in galaxies without SF in the tail the content within the stellar body tend to be normal/low in 50 (3/6) of the cases (magenta dots in Fig. fig:fh2 , representing detections with S/N > 3). If we include the low S/N detections, this percentage increases to 67 (6/9). Our result seems to suggest that the different gas proportion and displacement follow a temporal evolution: first the galaxy is hit by the ram pressure and starts developing the asymmetry, possibly losing molecular gas as well (at least the more diffuse component). At the same time, in the regions of the disc compressed by ram pressure the is efficiently converted in molecular gas in the disk, and by the time we see the ionized gas tail the molecular gas fraction of the galaxy is significantly enhanced. This increase in the molecular gas fraction seems to be preserved also in galaxies in advanced stripping stage, as is the case of A3376 JW108. This result is perfectly in agreement with the detection of large cold gas reservoir in post-starburst (PSB) galaxies French+2015,Rowlands+2015,Alatalo+2016 , under the hypothesis that these objects belong to the evolutionary sequence caused by the RPS GASPXXIV,Werle+2022 . In fact, in PSB galaxies the cold gas is thought to be present in a diffuse phase not contributing to the star formation. Molecular gas in the stripped tails The molecular gas content measured in the pointings covering the expected gaseous tail amount to 10 ^ 9 in all the three sources with S/N > 3, namely A3376 S64, A3558 S167 (JO147) and A85 S64 (JO200).

Interestingly, two of these tails belong to very massive GASP galaxies (JO200 and JO147) and exhibit a narrow CO linewidth ( 90 and 130 , respectively), while the third one is a low-mass galaxy showing a broad linewidth.

gmw_00003502.name: PGC023233
gmw_00003502.overview: PGC023233 is the central galaxy of the Abell 0644 cooling-flow cluster [arXiv:1401.1822]. The galaxy exhibits nuclear hydrogen emission [arXiv:1401.1822], intermediate-age stellar populations [arXiv:1401.1822], and weak star formation activity with no evidence of active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1401.1822].

sources.summary: The paper describes PGC023233 as an Abell 0644 central galaxy with nuclear H emission, mixed stellar-population fits, intermediate ages, and weak star-formation evidence from NUV data.

quotes.quote: PGC023233 -- Abell 0644 This host cluster is classified as a cooling-flow cluster (see Table table:objects ), and exhibited nuclear H emission in McDonald et al. \ (2010). For this galaxy, the CSP fitting resulted in an inconsistent number of components (several bins consisting of 1,2 or 3 components -- see Table table:CSP_PGC ), and the SSP fitting delivered an age estimate ranging between 6000 to 20 000 Myr (the upper limit of the model), with the majority of the bins being of intermediate age. Thus for this galaxy, it is difficult to accurately pinpoint the most probable age estimate and number of components. It can be seen in figure 6 in McDonald et al. \ (2010) that the upper limit of the global NUV luminosity of Abell 0644 (measured from archival GALEX images) lies barely above the star formation law (Kennicutt 1998). It is therefore not obviously younger or with more star formation activity than the other three galaxies analysed in this article.

From the GALEX NUV excess, Hicks, Mushotzky Donahue (2010) estimate a star formation rate of 0.004 M_ /yr (assuming continuous star formation over 20 Myr) for PGC023233 (with no evidence of AGN activity).

gmw_00003503.name: 2MASXJ17122774-2322108
gmw_00003503.overview: The brightest cluster galaxy 2MASXJ17122774-2322108 is a central cD galaxy [arXiv:0910.0025, arXiv:2002.01291] that hosts a low-level active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1604.01038, arXiv:0812.3598] and exhibits nuclear hydrogen emission [arXiv:1401.1822]. It is offset from the cluster's global center of mass [arXiv:1604.01038] and is surrounded by a diffuse radio mini-halo [arXiv:0910.0025, arXiv:2002.01291].

sources.summary: The Ophiuchus BCG is offset from the cluster center and pressure peak, while its compact central radio source shows low-level accretion without extended structure.

quotes.quote: Dynamical activity and the truncated cooling core The 38 kpc offset of the centroid of the large scale X-ray emission distribution from the central BCG and the prominent southern surface brightness excess indicate that the gravitational potential in the cluster core has been strongly perturbed. The maps of projected thermodynamic properties support this picture. In a galaxy cluster in approximate hydrostatic equilibrium, with subsonic gas motions, the pressure distribution traces the gravitational potential of the system. However, contrary to most cooling core clusters, the pressure distribution in the Ophiuchus cluster is highly azimuthally asymmetric around the BCG and displays a prominent region of excess pressure in the north. These results strongly indicate that the BCG, which presumably traces the dark matter density peak, is offset from the cluster's global center of mass.

Radio properties We detect a point-like radio source associated with the center of the BCG with a flux density of 30 mJy and spectral index =-0.55 . The source is both compact and optically thin, typical of very low level accretion. As can be seen from the left panel of Fig. radioim , we find no evidence of extended structure near the BCG above the rms level of 10 Jy. The upper limit on the polarization of the source is 0.2 per cent.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASX J17122774-2322108 as Ophiuchus's central cD galaxy, with peculiar velocity, a radio mini-halo, optical-X-ray offset, and stripped interstellar gas linked to ram pressure.

quotes.quote: The dominant cluster galaxy exhibits a substantial peculiar velocity, v_p= 650 km s ^ 1 , with respect to the cluster mean, cz=9045 30 km s ^ 1 (Wakamatsu 2005; Hasegawa 2000). The presence of many nearby, smaller clusters and groups argues that Ophiuchus lies at the center of a supercluster (Wakamatsu 2005; see also Hasegawa 2000). Govoni (2009; see also Murgia 2009) report the discovery of a low surface brightness, diffuse radio mini-halo surrounding the dominant cluster galaxy, 2MASX J17122774-2322108. The central cD galaxy is also detected at 1.4 GHz with a flux density of 29.3 mJy in the NVSS survey (Condon 1998; see also Section section:image ) with a 45 45 arcsec ^2 (FWHM) synthesized beam.

The dominant cluster galaxy is seen near the center of the field. The green circle in the figure marks the location of the X-ray peak; the radius of the circle provides a conservative estimate of the astrometric uncertainty in the X-ray astrometry ( 1 arcsec). As was also reported by P erez-Torres (2009), the optical centroid is offset to the south of the X-ray peak by 4 arcsec (2 kpc). This offset presumably reflects the impact of ram pressure in slowing down the X-ray emitting gas, and the essentially collisionless nature of the stars (and dark matter) associated with the galaxy, which carry on ahead.

sources.summary: The paper describes 2MASXJ17122774-2322108 as the Ophiuchus central galaxy, with nuclear H emission, intermediate and very young stellar components, radial young-star variation, and suspected AGN activity.

quotes.quote: 2MASXJ17122774-2322108 -- Ophiuchus This low density cluster was classified as a cooling-flow cluster (White et al. \ 1997), whereas McDonald et al. \ (2010) fit a null spectroscopic mass deposition rate to this cluster, The CCG showed nuclear H emission in McDonald et al. \ (2010). Tables table:SSP_2MAS and table:CSP_2MAS shows the SSP and CSP equivalent ages and metallicities derived for the galaxy. The ^ 2 values of the SSP and CSP fits do not differ significantly, but once again the multiple component fitting was found to be more consistent, and showed that the majority of the bins consisted of a larger intermediate component of 7000 Myr and a smaller very young component of 10 Myr which contributes up to 25 per cent of the light.

Only one galaxy (2MASXJ17122774-2322108) shows a decrease in the fraction of the young stars as the radius increase from the centre of the galaxy. The other galaxies show that the young stars are equally present throughout the radius that our observations cover. It is further necessary to take note of any possible AGN activity in the central galaxies, as the weak emission lines from AGN can fill in some of the absorption lines that is important for stellar population age measurements (such as the Balmer lines), and can ultimately lead to older derived age estimates. The total 1.4 GHz radio flux at the centre of a cluster may be indicative of AGN activity. For the four clusters we study here, we used the 1.4 GHz fluxes measured from the NRAO VLA Sky Survey (NVSS; Condon et al. \ 1998). All but one galaxy (PGC023233) show radio emission in Figures fig:ESO_NVSS to fig:2MAS_NVSS . The H filaments measured in McDonald et al. \ (2010) are very small ( < 4 kpc) in comparison with the extensive radio emission, but are located in the centres of the 1.4 GHz and X-ray emission. Previous optical emission-line and ultraviolet studies can then be included to quantitatively determine whether AGN contamination is present in the optical spectra. Three of the four galaxies (all except ESO541-013) have nuclear H emission. ESO541-013 is classified as a non-emission line galaxy by Edwards et al. \ (2007), as its H equivalent width is --0.08 0.09 (after correcting for the underlying absorption), whereas emission galaxies are classified with H equivalent width as > 0.5 . Using this same criteria, MCG-02-02-086 is classified as a galaxy with star forming activity as its H equivalent width is 1.13 0.21 (and no AGN activity is suspected). From the GALEX NUV excess, Hicks, Mushotzky Donahue (2010) estimate a star formation rate of 0.004 M_ /yr (assuming continuous star formation over 20 Myr) for PGC023233 (with no evidence of AGN activity). 2MASXJ17122774-2322108, however, is a suspected AGN (see Murgia et al. \ 2010 and Perez-Torres et al. \ 2009).

sources.summary: The paper links the Ophiuchus cluster central cD galaxy to a faint radio point source, displaced cool gas, current AGN starvation, and a past giant radio-bubble outburst.

quotes.quote: The cluster center coincides with the radio point source embedded in the diffuse minihalo and associated with the central cD galaxy (yellow cross in Fig. fig:images1 a ). The central diffuse minihalo (G09, M10) is well detected in our images, where it occupies an area of 200 kpc in radius. The location of the central cD galaxy (yellow cross) coincides with the faint radio point source embedded in the minihalo emission (M10, W16).

However, the central AGN could have been much more active in the past. The X-ray image shows that sloshing has currently displaced the peak of the cluster cool gas away from the cD galaxy, something that is rarely seen in clusters and that can temporarily starve the AGN of its accretion fuel (Hamer et al. 2012, W16). An exotic alternative is that the radio galaxy that gave rise to this bubble is not the cD galaxy, but some other galaxy perhaps in the cluster outskirts; the counterpart lobe would then fade quickly in the low-density gas far from the cluster center.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASX J17122774-2322108 as the Ophiuchus central cD galaxy hosting a flat-spectrum, low-level AGN associated with compact radio and X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: The location of this point source is only 3.5'' away from the elliptical cD galaxy 2MASX J17122774-2322108. Furthermore, Fig. fig,chandra top shows that the NVSS 1400 MHz radio peak of the radio emitting source A matches well with the location of the X-ray point source at the core of the Ophiuchus cluster. This spatial coincidence suggests that the radio emission from source A and the X-ray emission from the point source at the core of the Ophiuchus cluster are associated. The radio emission from source A (see Table 2) indicates a flat spectrum, which suggests that its radio emission is AGN-like, and thus the nuclear activity of the cD galaxy 2MASX J17122774-2322108 can be claimed responsible for the radio and X-ray emission.

gmw_00003504.name: SHARDS10000327
gmw_00003504.overview: SHARDS10000327 is an early-type E/S0 galaxy with a medium inclination [arXiv:1705.05850]. It features a Type-III profile with a distinct break located 5.4 kpc from its center, and it has no nearby galaxies [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SHARDS10000327 as an E/S0 galaxy with a medium-inclination Type-III profile, no nearby galaxies, a 5.4 kpc break, and distinct inner and outer profile peaks.

quotes.quote: SHARDS10000327: E/S0 galaxy with a Type-III profile, with no nearby galaxies, and medium inclination (see Table tab:fits_psforr ). The object shows a clear Type-III break at 5. 4 kpc from the centre.

gmw_00003505.name: SHARDS10000827
gmw_00003505.overview: SHARDS10000827 is a medium-inclination lenticular S0 galaxy that exhibits a Type-III profile [arXiv:1705.05850]. The galaxy is classified as an active galactic nucleus source and is located near a low-surface-brightness field object [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SHARDS10000827 as a medium-inclination Type-III S0 galaxy flagged as an AGN source, requiring aggressive masking because of a low-surface-brightness field object.

quotes.quote: SHARDS10000827: S0 galaxy with Type-III profile. The object presents a medium inclination (see Table tab:fits_psforr ). It was flagged as an AGN source (see Sect. Subsec:AGN ). A high level of masking was needed due to a low surface brightness field object to the SW.

gmw_00003506.name: SHARDS10000840
gmw_00003506.overview: SHARDS10000840 is an early-type E/S0 galaxy with a medium-low inclination [arXiv:1705.05850]. Although two edge-on field galaxies are located nearby to the northeast, SHARDS10000840 displays undisturbed isophotes and a Type-III profile [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SHARDS10000840 as an E/S0 Type-III galaxy with medium-low inclination, aggressive masking near two edge-on field galaxies, undisturbed isophotes, and separated profile peaks.

quotes.quote: SHARDS10000840: E/S0 galaxy with Type-III profile. The object presents a medium to low inclination (see Table tab:fits_psforr ). The image needed an aggressive level of masking due to two nearby edge on field galaxies to the NE, including manual masking and revision of the whole FoV. We do not find any significant perturbations in the isophotes that could be due to the field objects. The PDDs for h and _ 0 show two clearly separated peaks corresponding to the two profiles.

gmw_00003507.name: SHARDS10000849
gmw_00003507.overview: SHARDS10000849 is a lenticular S0 galaxy with a medium-to-high inclination [arXiv:1705.05850]. It exhibits a Type-III profile, and its outer profile is skewed despite neighboring sources being far enough away to avoid distorting the main structure [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SHARDS10000849 as a medium-high-inclination Type-III S0 galaxy whose masked neighboring sources do not distort the profile, though the outer profile is skewed.

quotes.quote: SHARDS10000849: S0 galaxy with Type-III profile. The object presents medium to high inclination (see Table tab:fits_psforr ). We have applied manual masking to two main sources in the FoV and a smaller one. Nevertheless, none of them appears to be close enough to distort the profile and any contribution to the outer parts of our profile was discarded.

gmw_00003508.name: SHARDS10001344
gmw_00003508.overview: SHARDS10001344 is a medium-inclination S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1705.05850]. It features a bright central component contrasted against a low-surface-brightness disk structure [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper describes SHARDS10001344 as a medium-inclination Type-III S0 galaxy with a bright central component, low-surface-brightness disk, extensive masking, and separated profile peaks.

quotes.quote: SHARDS10001344: S0 Type-III galaxy with medium inclination (see Table tab:fits_psforr ) and a very bright central component ( _ R = 16 ) in contrast with the low surface brightness of the disc structure. We applied an extensive masking to a nearby region to the N-EE and a small close source to the N-NW. The PDDs for h and _ 0 show two separated peaks corresponding to the two profiles. The PDDs of the inner disc profile appear with two peaks and distorted due to the low statistics that result from the aggresive masking performed to the central bulge to avoid contamination.

gmw_00003509.name: SHARDS10002730
gmw_00003509.overview: SHARDS10002730 is a small lenticular S0 galaxy characterized by a medium inclination [arXiv:1705.05850]. It exhibits a Type-III profile with an excess of light in its outermost region that remains statistically meaningful after point spread function subtraction [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper describes SHARDS10002730 as a small, medium-inclination Type-III S0 whose outer excess remains statistically significant after PSF subtraction despite extensive masking.

quotes.quote: SHARDS10002730: Small S0 galaxy with Type-III profile. It has a medium inclination (see Table tab:fits_psforr ). It required extensive masking, but no masked pixel finally was inside the fitting region. The automated break analysis reveals that the excess of light shown at the outermost part of the galaxy is statistically significant as a Type-III break despite the PSF subtraction, although the the probability of being a Type I is p 0.

gmw_00003510.name: SHARDS10002942
gmw_00003510.overview: SHARDS10002942 is a face-on lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1705.05850]. Its structure consists of a bulge and a Type-III disk, which exhibits an excess of light in its outermost region that remains after point spread function subtraction [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SHARDS10002942 as a face-on Type-III S0 galaxy whose masked profile shows a bulge plus Type-III disk, with outer excess significant after PSF subtraction.

quotes.quote: SHARDS10002942: Type-III S0 galaxy with face on orientation (see Table tab:fits_psforr ). Multiple and extensive masking was required due to the presence of nearby objects. The profile shows a bulge and Type-III disc composition, as the small excess of light at the outermost part of the galaxy is proved to be statistically significant by the automatic break analysis after the PSF subtraction.

gmw_00003511.name: SHARDS10003312
gmw_00003511.overview: SHARDS10003312 is an early-type E/S0 galaxy with a medium inclination [arXiv:1705.05850]. The galaxy is almost isolated and exhibits a Type-III profile characterized by two distinct exponential profiles [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SHARDS10003312 as an almost isolated, medium-inclination E/S0 Type-III galaxy whose automated break analysis finds two exponential profiles with separated distributions.

quotes.quote: SHARDS10003312: E/S0 galaxy with Type-III profile. It has a medium inclination (see Table tab:fits_psforr ) and it is almost isolated, so no manual masking was needed. The automated break analysis successfully detected two exponential profiles, with narrow and separated PDDs, although the distributions associated with the outer profile presented higher dispersions due to the irregularities found in the profile.

gmw_00003512.name: SHARDS10003647
gmw_00003512.overview: SHARDS10003647 is a medium-inclination S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1705.05850]. It features a Type-III profile with a break and a wavy outer disk structure [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SHARDS10003647 as a medium-inclination Type-III S0 galaxy with masked field objects, a wavy outer disk profile, separated profile distributions, and a significant break.

quotes.quote: SHARDS10003647: S0 galaxy with a Type-III profile. It shows a medium inclination (see Table tab:fits_psforr ). Manual masking was required to avoid contamination from small field objects.

The outermost region of the disc profile seems to be wavy, distorting the PDDs. The PDD of h and _ 0 appear to be clearly separated for the inner and outer profiles, although the outer profile is clearly skewed and wider than the inner one. The object present a significant Type-III profile despite the wide distribution of the outer profile.

gmw_00003513.name: SHARDS10009610
gmw_00003513.overview: SHARDS10009610 is classified as an S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1705.05850]. It exhibits a Type-III profile characterized by clearly separated inner and outer profile peaks [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SHARDS10009610 as a Type-III S0 galaxy whose masking avoided final fitting contamination and whose inner and outer profile peaks are clearly separated.

quotes.quote: SHARDS10009610: S0 galaxy with a Type-III profile. Manual masking was applied to multiple sources, but none of them finally laid within in the final fitting region. The PDDs for h and _ 0 show two clearly separated peaks corresponding to the inner and outer profiles. The outer profile PDD seems to be slightly wider than the inner one due to little distortions at high radius.

gmw_00003514.name: SHARDS20000593
gmw_00003514.overview: SHARDS20000593 is classified as a Type-III S0 lenticular galaxy characterized by a very bright central bulge and low ellipticity [arXiv:1705.05850]. Its surface brightness profile features an exponential component, with an excess of emission extending beyond a radius of 6 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SHARDS20000593 as a Type-III S0 with a bright bulge, low ellipticity, prominent exponential component, significant excess beyond 6 kpc, and separated profile peaks.

quotes.quote: SHARDS20000593: Type-III S0 galaxy. The central bulge is very bright, and the ellipticity is low, but there is a prominent exponential component in the surface brightness profile that led us to classify it as S0, instead of elliptical. Extensive masking in the low emission regions. The disc profile presents a signficative excess of emission beyond R 6 kpc with respect to the inner exponential section of the disc that cannot be explained by PSF contribution. The PDDs for h and _ 0 show two clearly separated peaks corresponding to the two profiles.

gmw_00003515.name: SHARDS20000827
gmw_00003515.overview: SHARDS20000827 is a highly inclined Type-III S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1705.05850]. Its light profile displays an excess of emission beyond a radius of 10 kpc that cannot be explained by point spread function contribution [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SHARDS20000827 as a highly inclined Type-III S0 analyzed with ISOFIT, with significant excess emission beyond 10 kpc unexplained by PSF contribution.

quotes.quote: SHARDS20000827: Type-III S0 galaxy. It is highly inclined (see Table tab:fits_psforr ) so it was analysed with ISOFIT instead of Ellipse . The profile presents a signficative excess of emission since R 10 kpc that cannot be explained by PSF contribution. The PDDs for h and _ 0 show two clearly separated peaks corresponding to the two profiles, although they present large uncertainities for the outer profile fit.

gmw_00003516.name: SHARDS20003210
gmw_00003516.overview: SHARDS20003210 is a face-on S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1705.05850]. Its structural profile reveals a clear bulge and a double-exponential structure with two separated peaks [arXiv:1705.05850].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SHARDS20003210 as a face-on Type-III S0 galaxy whose profile reveals a clear bulge plus double-exponential structure with separated profile peaks.

quotes.quote: SHARDS20003210: S0 galaxy with a Type-III profile. The object presents a face-on orientation (see Table tab:fits_psforr ). The profile reveal a clear bulge + double exponential structure. The PDDs for h and _ 0 show two clearly separated peaks corresponding to the two profiles.

gmw_00003517.name: SMM J14009+0252
gmw_00003517.overview: SMM J14009+0252 is a background submillimeter galaxy magnified by the gravitational lensing of the Abell 1835 cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0703387, arXiv:1005.4699]. It is classified as an active galactic nucleus host [arXiv:1207.0492] and contains a warm gas phase [arXiv:1207.0492].

sources.summary: The paper treats A1835- 2 as an extremely red submillimeter galaxy, favoring z around 3, high extinction, faint K-band flux, and possible AGN contribution.

quotes.quote: From the near-IR to submm SED and from the low value of ^ 850 _ 1.4 , Ivison \ (2000) argue that A1835- 2 is more likely at 3 z 5 and that the radio flux contains some AGN contribution. A recent reanalysis of the radio and submm flux by Aretxaga \ (2003) yields a higher redshift estimate of z 4.1 0.8 However, their best fit SED strongly overpredicts the observed near- to mid-IR flux. Our earlier photometric redshift estimate of z 1.2--1.6 (Richard \ 2006) is now superseded by the present analysis including in particular the longer wavelength IRAC/Spitzer observations leading to a higher z .

Compared to other SCUBA galaxies studied also in the rest-frame optical (cf. Smail \ 2004) A1835- 2 fares among the faintest ones in K and among the ``reddest ones'' in optical/IR flux. With m(Ks) 20.5 it is close to the faintest objects of Smail , which have 18 Ks 20.9 ; among the 7 confirmed sub-mm galaxies observed observed in the SCUBA Cluster Lens Survey of Frayer \ (2004) it is the second faintest object in K surpassed only by SMMJ00266+1708 with m(K) = 22.36 0.16 , and the second reddest in J-K . After lensing correction the magnitude of A1835- 2 is m(Ks) 21.2 . Several other sub-mm galaxies are known with very faint flux levels at K 21.0--21.9 (Smail \ 2002, Dannerbauer \ 2002). Its restframe V-band to IR luminosity ratio is very low, 5. 10^ 4 , placing it among the 5 most extreme sub-mm galaxies when compared to the Smail \ (2004) sample. In terms of stellar populations we find a dominant stellar age of 0.36 Gyr or younger for A1835- 2, similar to the mean ages of (310-530) (80-90) Myr estimated by Smail \ (2004) for a sample of sub-mm galaxies and optically faint radio galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper models SMM J14009+0252 as one of two lensed background submillimeter galaxies in the Abell 1835 field and estimates its 1.1 mm flux at about 11.3 mJy.

quotes.quote: The data is well described by a model including an extended Sunyaev-Zel'dovich (SZ) signal from the cluster gas plus emission from two bright background submm galaxies magnified by the gravitational lensing of the cluster. The best-fit values for the central Compton value for the cluster and the fluxes of the two main point sources in the field: SMM J140104+0252, and SMM J14009+0252 are found to be y_ 0 =(4.34 0.52 0.69) 10^ 4 , 6.5 2.0 0.7 mJy and 11.3 1.9 1.1 mJy, where the first error represents the statistical measurement error and the second error represents the estimated systematic error in the result.

A parameter search has been used to determine the best fit values of the central Compton parameter for the cluster and the fluxes for the point sources SMM J140104+0252 and SMM J14009+0252. These values are found to be y_ 0 =(4.68 0.48 0.82) 10^ 4 , 6.5 2.0 mJy and 11.3 1.9 mJy, respectively with a statistical signal-to-noise of 10.5, 3.3 and 6.0 respectively.

sources.summary: The paper observes lensed SMM J14009+0252 in CO and radio continuum, finding warm gas excitation, steep radio spectrum, and AGN classification.

quotes.quote: Here, we follow this familiar pattern. We exploit the recent upgrade to the National Radio Astronomy Observatory's (NRAO's 1 ) Karl G. \ Jansky Very Large Array (VLA), which includes the provision of Ka-band receivers (26.5--40 GHz), to study the redshifted \ \ emission from three lensed SMGs: (i) SMM J14009+0252 lying at z=2.9344 ivison00, weiss09 , lensed by Abell 1835 and one of the brightest SMGs detected in the SCUBA Cluster Lens Survey SCLS; smail02 (ii) SMM J16359+6612, a bright SMG a z=2.5168 kneib04, kneib05 and lensed into three images by the Abell 2218 cluster, and (iii) SMM J02399--0136 at z=2.808 ivison98 , the first SCUBA galaxy and the brightest of the SCLS galaxies, lensed by Abell 370.
SMM J14009 too has long been suspected to host a dust-enshrouded AGN on the basis of the strength of its radio emission ( S_ 1.4 GHz = 529 30 Jy) and compactness Biggs \ in prep. ivison00 .

Our improved sampling of the radio SED of SMM J14009 allows us to determine that this source also has a steep radio continuum spectrum ( subsect:radio-continuum ), corroborating its classification as an AGN. Additionally, SMM J14009 demonstrates a high T_ b ratio, r_ (3-2)/(1-0) = 0.95 0.12 , which indicates the presence of a warm gas phase given the excitation requirements of the \ \ transition, n_ crit 10^4 cm ^ 3 , E_ u /k_ B 33 k .

gmw_00003518.name: AGC 114905
gmw_00003518.overview: AGC 114905 is a gas-rich ultra-diffuse dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2112.00017]. High-resolution observations reveal a regular, rotating hydrogen disc with a low-amplitude rotation curve that can be explained almost entirely by the galaxy's baryonic mass distribution alone [arXiv:2112.00017].

sources.summary: The paper presents high-resolution H i observations of AGC 114905, finding a regular rotating disc whose low-amplitude rotation curve is explained by baryons but challenges standard CDM and MOND expectations.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We present new H i interferometric observations of the gas-rich ultra-diffuse galaxy AGC 114905, which previous work, based on low-resolution data, identified as an outlier of the baryonic Tully-Fisher relation. The new observations, at a spatial resolution 2.5 times higher than before, reveal a regular H i disc rotating at about 23 km s ^ 1 . Our kinematic parameters, recovered with a robust 3D kinematic modelling fitting technique, show that the flat part of the rotation curve is reached.

Intriguingly, the rotation curve can be explained almost entirely by the baryonic mass distribution alone. We show that a standard cold dark matter halo that follows the concentration-halo mass relation fails to reproduce the amplitude of the rotation curve by a large margin. Only a halo with an extremely (and arguably unfeasible) low concentration reaches agreement with the data.

gmw_00003519.name: PKS 0150 334
gmw_00003519.overview: PKS 0150 334 is an active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of 0.61 [arXiv:1701.01172]. High-resolution imaging reveals a core-jet morphology with two components that exhibit apparent superluminal motion [arXiv:1701.01172].

sources.summary: PKS 0150 334 is imaged as two components whose changing separation and apparent superluminal motion make the authors favor a core-jet morphology over a compact double.

quotes.quote: PKS 0150 334: Imaged at 5 GHz as part of the VLBApls observations in June 1996 and resolved into two components of similar brightness separated by 1 mas . While at face value this is suggestive of a compact double, the spacing is somewhat less than would be typical for this class (cf. \ Snellen et al. \ 2000) and a core-jet morphology cannot be ruled out.
